revision.comment: [[WP:AES|←]]Created page with ''''Yūki Shrine''' (北畠神社, ''Yūki jinja'') is a [[Shinto]] shrine located in [[Tsu, Mie]] Prefecture, Japan. Its main festival is held annually on May 1,...'
revision.text:
'''Yūki Shrine''' (北畠神社, ''Yūki jinja'') is a [[Shinto]] shrine located in [[Tsu, Mie]] Prefecture, Japan. Its main festival is held annually on May 1, 2 and 3. It was founded in 1879, and enshrines the [[kami]] of [[Yūki Munehiro]]. It is one of the [[Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]].
==See also== *[[Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]]
==External links== *[http://www.kankomie.or.jp/spot/detail_2924.html Prefectural tourism website]
{{DEFAULTSORT:Yūki Shrine}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Shinto-stub}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Shinto shrines]] [[Category:1879 establishments]] [[ja:北畠神社]]

'''Yūki Shrine''' (結城神社, ''Yūki jinja'') is a [[Shinto]] shrine located in [[Tsu, Mie]] Prefecture, Japan. Its main festival is held annually on May 1, 2 and 3. It was founded in 1879, and enshrines the [[kami]] of [[Yūki Munehiro]]. It is one of the [[Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]].
==See also== *[[Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]]
==External links== *[http://www.kankomie.or.jp/spot/detail_2924.html Prefectural tourism website]
{{DEFAULTSORT:Yūki Shrine}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Shinto-stub}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Shinto shrines]] [[Category:1879 establishments]] [[ja:結城神社]]

revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building | name = Yūki Shrine
結城神社 | image = Yuukizinnjya.jpg | caption = ''Heiden'' of Yūki Shrine | map_type = Japan Mie Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34.6977|136.5152|type:landmark_region:JP|format=dms|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Yūki Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] | deity = [[Yūki Munehiro]] | founded_by = | established =1824, restored 1879 | date_destroyed = | location = 2341 Fujikata, Tsu City, Mie Prefecture | website = | architecture_style = | festival = May 1-3 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Yūki Shrine'''|結城神社|Yūki jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the city of [[Tsu, Mie|Tsu]], [[Mie Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. Its main festival is held annually on May 1, 2 and 3. It is one of the [[Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]]. ==History== [[Yūki Munehiro]] (1266 – 1339) was a vassal of the [[Kamakura shogunate]] who had been entrusted with southern [[Mutsu Province]] and was castellan of [[Shirakawa Castle]] He was dispatched by [[Hōjō Takatoki]] to [[Kyoto]] in 1331 during the [[Genkō War]], but subsequently defected to the side of [[Emperor Go-Daigo]]. He returned as one of [[Nitta Yoshisada]]'s generals during the 1333 [[Battle of Kamakura]] to overthrow the [[Hōjō clan| Hōjō]]. Following the [[Kenmu Restoration]], he served alongside [[Kusunoki Masashige]] as an aide to Emperor Go-Daigo and assisted [[Kitabatake Akiie]] conquer Mutsu Province. When [[Ashikaga Takauji]] rebelled against Emperor Go-Daigo, he led the army which retook Kyoto from the Ashikaga in 1336 and was awarded a sword from the emperor. However, in 1338 Ashikaga Takauji retook Kyoto, and Yūki Munehiro was forced to flee to [[Yoshino, Nara]] where Emperor Go-Daigo had established the [[Southern Court]]. He subsequently crossed into [[Ise Province]] and attempted to take a boat back to Mutsu to rally his forces against the Ashikaga, but was shipwrecked and died.{{Cite book |last=Sansom |first=George |title=A History of Japan, 1334-1615 |publisher=Stanford University Press |year=1961 |ISBN=0804705259 |page=43,48,66}} In 1824, [[Tōdō Takasawa]], the ''[[daimyō]]'' of [[Tsu Domain]] erected a small shrine on the site of his grave. Following the [[Meiji Restoration]] and the establishment of [[State Shinto]], the [[Meiji government]] wished to sponsor patriotism by recalling the events and people of the previous Kenmu Restoration. Yūki Shrine was given the official status of a village shrine in 1879 under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]]. [[Emperor Meiji]] visited Mie Prefecture the following year and made an offering of 200 [[Yen]] to the shrine. The shrine was promoted to special status in 1882 as one of the "Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration". The shrine was burned down in July 1945 during World War II, and was reconstructed in the late 1950s. It is located approximately one kilometer southeast of [[Akogi Station]] on the [[Kisei Main Line]]. ==See also== *[[Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== *[http://www.kankomie.or.jp/spot/detail_2924.html Mie Prefectural tourism website] *[https://www.tsukanko.jp/spot/18/ Tsu city tourism website] {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Yuki Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:1879 establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Tsu, Mie]] {{Shinto-stub}}

The shrine was promoted to special status in 1882 as one of the "Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration". The shrine was burned down in July 1945 during World War II, and was reconstructed in the late 1950s. It is located approximately one kilometer southeast of [[Akogi Station]] on the [[Kisei Main Line]]. ==See also== *[[Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== *[http://www.kankomie.or.jp/spot/detail_2924.html Mie Prefectural tourism website] *[https://www.tsukanko.jp/spot/18/ Tsu city tourism website] {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Yuki Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:1879 establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Tsu, Mie]] {{Shinto-stub}}

revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building | name = Yūki Shrine
結城神社 | image = Yuukizinnjya.jpg | caption = ''Heiden'' of Yūki Shrine | map_type = Japan Mie Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34.6977|136.5152|type:landmark_region:JP|format=dms|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Yūki Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] | deity = [[Yūki Munehiro]] | founded_by = | established =1824, restored 1879 | date_destroyed = | location = 2341 Fujikata, Tsu City, Mie Prefecture | website = | architecture_style = | festival = May 1-3 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Yūki Shrine'''|結城神社|Yūki jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the city of [[Tsu, Mie|Tsu]], [[Mie Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. Its main festival is held annually on May 1, 2 and 3. It is one of the [[Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]]. ==History== [[Yūki Munehiro]] (1266 – 1339) was a vassal of the [[Kamakura shogunate]] who had been entrusted with southern [[Mutsu Province]] and was castellan of [[Shirakawa Castle]] He was dispatched by [[Hōjō Takatoki]] to [[Kyoto]] in 1331 during the [[Genkō War]], but subsequently defected to the side of [[Emperor Go-Daigo]]. He returned as one of [[Nitta Yoshisada]]'s generals during the 1333 [[Siege of Kamakura (1333)|Battle of Kamakura]] to overthrow the [[Hōjō clan| Hōjō]]. Following the [[Kenmu Restoration]], he served alongside [[Kusunoki Masashige]] as an aide to Emperor Go-Daigo and assisted [[Kitabatake Akiie]] conquer Mutsu Province. When [[Ashikaga Takauji]] rebelled against Emperor Go-Daigo, he led the army which retook Kyoto from the Ashikaga in 1336 and was awarded a sword from the emperor. However, in 1338 Ashikaga Takauji retook Kyoto, and Yūki Munehiro was forced to flee to [[Yoshino, Nara]] where Emperor Go-Daigo had established the [[Southern Court]]. He subsequently crossed into [[Ise Province]] and attempted to take a boat back to Mutsu to rally his forces against the Ashikaga, but was shipwrecked and died.{{Cite book |last=Sansom |first=George |title=A History of Japan, 1334-1615 |publisher=Stanford University Press |year=1961 |ISBN=0804705259 |page=43,48,66}} In 1824, [[Tōdō Takasawa]], the ''[[daimyō]]'' of [[Tsu Domain]] erected a small shrine on the site of his grave. Following the [[Meiji Restoration]] and the establishment of [[State Shinto]], the [[Meiji government]] wished to sponsor patriotism by recalling the events and people of the previous Kenmu Restoration. Yūki Shrine was given the official status of a village shrine in 1879 under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]]. [[Emperor Meiji]] visited Mie Prefecture the following year and made an offering of 200 [[Yen]] to the shrine. The shrine was promoted to special status in 1882 as one of the "Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration". The shrine was burned down in July 1945 during World War II, and was reconstructed in the late 1950s. It is located approximately one kilometer southeast of [[Akogi Station]] on the [[Kisei Main Line]]. ==See also== *[[Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== *[http://www.kankomie.or.jp/spot/detail_2924.html Mie Prefectural tourism website] *[https://www.tsukanko.jp/spot/18/ Tsu city tourism website] {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Yuki Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:1879 establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Tsu, Mie]] [[Category:Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] [[Category:Bekkaku Kanpeisha]] [[Category:Shinto]][[en:Yūki Shrine]] {{Shinto-stub}}

It is located approximately one kilometer southeast of [[Akogi Station]] on the [[Kisei Main Line]]. ==See also== *[[Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== *[http://www.kankomie.or.jp/spot/detail_2924.html Mie Prefectural tourism website] *[https://www.tsukanko.jp/spot/18/ Tsu city tourism website] {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Yuki Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:1879 establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Tsu, Mie]] [[Category:Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] [[Category:Bekkaku Kanpeisha]] [[Category:Uncategorized_Shinto_shrines]][[en:Yūki Shrine]][[Category:Uncategorized_Shinto_shrines]] [[Category:Shinto]][[en:Yūki Shrine]] {{Shinto-stub}}

He returned as one of [[Nitta Yoshisada]]'s generals during the 1333 [[Siege of Kamakura (1333)|Battle of Kamakura]] to overthrow the [[Hōjō clan| Hōjō]]. Following the [[Kenmu Restoration]], he served alongside [[Kusunoki Masashige]] as an aide to Emperor Go-Daigo and assisted [[Kitabatake Akiie]] conquer Mutsu Province. When [[Ashikaga Takauji]] rebelled against Emperor Go-Daigo, he led the army which retook Kyoto from the Ashikaga in 1336 and was awarded a sword from the emperor. However, in 1338 Ashikaga Takauji retook Kyoto, and Yūki Munehiro was forced to flee to [[Yoshino, Nara]] where Emperor Go-Daigo had established the [[Southern Court]]. He subsequently crossed into [[Ise Province]] and attempted to take a boat back to Mutsu to rally his forces against the Ashikaga, but was shipwrecked and died.{{Cite book |last=Sansom |first=George |title=A History of Japan, 1334-1615 |publisher=Stanford University Press |year=1961 |ISBN=0804705259 |page=43,48,66}} In 1824, [[Tōdō Takasawa]], the ''[[daimyō]]'' of [[Tsu Domain]] erected a small shrine on the site of his grave. The shrine was promoted to special status in 1882 as one of the "Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration". The shrine was burned down in July 1945 during World War II, and was reconstructed in the late 1950s. {{Infobox religious building | name = Yūki Shrine
結城神社 | image = Yuukizinnjya.jpg | caption = ''Heiden'' of Yūki Shrine | map_type = Japan Mie Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34.6977|136.5152|type:landmark_region:JP|format=dms|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Yūki Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] | deity = [[Yūki Munehiro]] | founded_by = | established =1824, restored 1879 | date_destroyed = | location = 2341 Fujikata, Tsu City, Mie Prefecture | website = | architecture_style = | festival = May 1-3 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Yūki Shrine'''|結城神社|Yūki jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the city of [[Tsu, Mie|Tsu]], [[Mie Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. Its main festival is held annually on May 1, 2 and 3. It is one of the [[Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]]. ==History== [[Yūki Munehiro]] (1266 – 1339) was a vassal of the [[Kamakura shogunate]] who had been entrusted with southern [[Mutsu Province]] and was castellan of [[Shirakawa Castle]] He was dispatched by [[Hōjō Takatoki]] to [[Kyoto]] in 1331 during the [[Genkō War]], but subsequently defected to the side of [[Emperor Go-Daigo]]. He returned as one of [[Nitta Yoshisada]]'s generals during the 1333 [[Siege of Kamakura (1333)|Battle of Kamakura]] to overthrow the [[Hōjō clan| Hōjō]]. Following the [[Kenmu Restoration]], he served alongside [[Kusunoki Masashige]] as an aide to Emperor Go-Daigo and assisted [[Kitabatake Akiie]] conquer Mutsu Province. When [[Ashikaga Takauji]] rebelled against Emperor Go-Daigo, he led the army which retook Kyoto from the Ashikaga in 1336 and was awarded a sword from the emperor. However, in 1338 Ashikaga Takauji retook Kyoto, and Yūki Munehiro was forced to flee to [[Yoshino, Nara]] where Emperor Go-Daigo had established the [[Southern Court]]. He subsequently crossed into [[Ise Province]] and attempted to take a boat back to Mutsu to rally his forces against the Ashikaga, but was shipwrecked and died.{{Cite book |last=Sansom |first=George |title=A History of Japan, 1334-1615 |publisher=Stanford University Press |year=1961 |ISBN=0804705259 |page=43,48,66}} In 1824, [[Tōdō Takasawa]], the ''[[daimyō]]'' of [[Tsu Domain]] erected a small shrine on the site of his grave. Following the [[Meiji Restoration]] and the establishment of [[State Shinto]], the [[Meiji government]] wished to sponsor patriotism by recalling the events and people of the previous Kenmu Restoration. Yūki Shrine was given the official status of a village shrine in 1879 under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]]. [[Emperor Meiji]] visited Mie Prefecture the following year and made an offering of 200 [[Yen]] to the shrine. The shrine was promoted to special status in 1882 as one of the "Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration". The shrine was burned down in July 1945 during World War II, and was reconstructed in the late 1950s. It is located approximately one kilometer southeast of [[Akogi Station]] on the [[Kisei Main Line]]. ==See also== *[[Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== *[http://www.kankomie.or.jp/spot/detail_2924.html Mie Prefectural tourism website] *[https://www.tsukanko.jp/spot/18/ Tsu city tourism website] {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Yuki Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:1879 establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Tsu, Mie]] [[Category:Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia35]] [[en:Yūki Shrine]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia35]] [[Category:Bekkaku Kanpeisha]] [[en:Yūki Shrine]][[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia35]] [[en:Yūki Shrine]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia25]] [[Category:dd]] [[en:Yūki Shrine]][[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia35]] [[en:Yūki Shrine]][[en:Yūki Shrine]] {{Shinto-stub}}

The shrine was burned down in July 1945 during World War II, and was reconstructed in the late 1950s. It is located approximately one kilometer southeast of [[Akogi Station]] on the [[Kisei Main Line]]. ==See also== *[[Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== *[http://www.kankomie.or.jp/spot/detail_2924.html Mie Prefectural tourism website] *[https://www.tsukanko.jp/spot/18/ Tsu city tourism website] {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Yuki Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:1879 establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Tsu, Mie]] [[Category:Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[en:Yūki Shrine]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[Category:Bekkaku Kanpeisha]] [[en:Yūki Shrine]][[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[en:Yūki Shrine]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[Category:dd]] [[en:Yūki Shrine]][[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[en:Yūki Shrine]][[en:Yūki Shrine]] {{Shinto-stub}}

However, in 1338 Ashikaga Takauji retook Kyoto, and Yūki Munehiro was forced to flee to [[Yoshino, Nara]] where Emperor Go-Daigo had established the [[Southern Court]]. He subsequently crossed into [[Ise Province]] and attempted to take a boat back to Mutsu to rally his forces against the Ashikaga, but was shipwrecked and died.{{Cite book |last=Sansom |first=George |title=A History of Japan, 1334-1615 |publisher=Stanford University Press |year=1961 |ISBN=0804705259 |page=43,48,66}} In 1824, [[Tōdō Takasawa]], the ''[[daimyō]]'' of [[Tsu Domain]] erected a small shrine on the site of his grave. Following the [[Meiji Restoration]] and the establishment of [[State Shinto]], the [[Meiji government]] wished to sponsor patriotism by recalling the events and people of the previous Kenmu Restoration. Yūki Shrine was given the official status of a village shrine in 1879 under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]]. [[Emperor Meiji]] visited Mie Prefecture the following year and made an offering of 200 [[Yen]] to the shrine. The shrine was promoted to special status in 1882 as one of the "Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration". The shrine was burned down in July 1945 during World War II, and was reconstructed in the late 1950s. It is located approximately one kilometer southeast of [[Akogi Station]] on the [[Kisei Main Line]]. ==See also== *[[Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== *[http://www.kankomie.or.jp/spot/detail_2924.html Mie Prefectural tourism website] *[https://www.tsukanko.jp/spot/18/ Tsu city tourism website] {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Yuki Shrine}} [[category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[category:1879 establishments in Japan]] [[category:Tsu, Mie]] [[category:Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] [[category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[en:Yūki Shrine]][[category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[category:Bekkaku Kanpeisha]] [[en:Yūki Shrine]][[category:Shinto]] [[category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[en:Yūki Shrine]][[category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[en:Yūki Shrine]][[category:Shinto]] [[category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[Category:ファイル]] [[en:Yūki Shrine]][[en:Yūki Shrine]] {{Shinto-stub}}

It is located approximately one kilometer southeast of [[Akogi Station]] on the [[Kisei Main Line]]. ==See also== *[[Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== *[http://www.kankomie.or.jp/spot/detail_2924.html Mie Prefectural tourism website] *[https://www.tsukanko.jp/spot/18/ Tsu city tourism website] {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]][[en:Yūki Shrine]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Yuki Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:1879 establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Tsu, Mie]] [[Category:Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 7]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Yūki Shrine}][[Category:qq]] [[en:Yūki Shrine]]]] [[en:Yūki Shrine]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 7]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Yūki Shrine}][[Category:qq]] [[en:Yūki Shrine]]]] [[Category:Bekkaku Kanpeisha]] [[en:Yūki Shrine]][[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 7]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Yūki Shrine}][[Category:qq]] [[en:Yūki Shrine]]]] [[en:Yūki Shrine]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 7]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Yūki Shrine}][[Category:qq]] [[en:Yūki Shrine]]]] [[en:Yūki Shrine]][[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 7]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Yūki Shrine}][[Category:qq]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:Yūki Shrine]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Yūki Shrine]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:Bekkaku-sha]] [[en:Yūki Shrine]]]] [[en:Yūki Shrine]][[en:Yūki Shrine]] {{Shinto-stub}}

revision.comment: Copying from [[Category:Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] to [[Category:qq-{{subst:NAMESPACE}}-qq]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building | name = Yūki Shrine
結城神社 | image = Yuukizinnjya.jpg | caption = ''Heiden'' of Yūki Shrine | map_type = Japan Mie Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34.6977|136.5152|type:landmark_region:JP|format=dms|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Yūki Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] | deity = [[Yūki Munehiro]] | founded_by = | established =1824, restored 1879 | date_destroyed = | location = 2341 Fujikata, Tsu City, Mie Prefecture | website = | architecture_style = | festival = May 1-3 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Yūki Shrine'''|結城神社|Yūki jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the city of [[Tsu, Mie|Tsu]], [[Mie Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. Its main festival is held annually on May 1, 2 and 3. It is one of the [[Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]]. ==History== [[Yūki Munehiro]] (1266 – 1339) was a vassal of the [[Kamakura shogunate]] who had been entrusted with southern [[Mutsu Province]] and was castellan of [[Shirakawa Castle]] He was dispatched by [[Hōjō Takatoki]] to [[Kyoto]] in 1331 during the [[Genkō War]], but subsequently defected to the side of [[Emperor Go-Daigo]]. He returned as one of [[Nitta Yoshisada]]'s generals during the 1333 [[Siege of Kamakura (1333)|Battle of Kamakura]] to overthrow the [[Hōjō clan| Hōjō]]. Following the [[Kenmu Restoration]], he served alongside [[Kusunoki Masashige]] as an aide to Emperor Go-Daigo and assisted [[Kitabatake Akiie]] conquer Mutsu Province. When [[Ashikaga Takauji]] rebelled against Emperor Go-Daigo, he led the army which retook Kyoto from the Ashikaga in 1336 and was awarded a sword from the emperor. However, in 1338 Ashikaga Takauji retook Kyoto, and Yūki Munehiro was forced to flee to [[Yoshino, Nara]] where Emperor Go-Daigo had established the [[Southern Court]]. He subsequently crossed into [[Ise Province]] and attempted to take a boat back to Mutsu to rally his forces against the Ashikaga, but was shipwrecked and died.{{Cite book |last=Sansom |first=George |title=A History of Japan, 1334-1615 |publisher=Stanford University Press |year=1961 |ISBN=0804705259 |page=43,48,66}} In 1824, [[Tōdō Takasawa]], the ''[[daimyō]]'' of [[Tsu Domain]] erected a small shrine on the site of his grave. Following the [[Meiji Restoration]] and the establishment of [[State Shinto]], the [[Meiji government]] wished to sponsor patriotism by recalling the events and people of the previous Kenmu Restoration. Yūki Shrine was given the official status of a village shrine in 1879 under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]]. [[Emperor Meiji]] visited Mie Prefecture the following year and made an offering of 200 [[Yen]] to the shrine. The shrine was promoted to special status in 1882 as one of the "Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration". The shrine was burned down in July 1945 during World War II, and was reconstructed in the late 1950s. It is located approximately one kilometer southeast of [[Akogi Station]] on the [[Kisei Main Line]]. ==See also== *[[Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== *[http://www.kankomie.or.jp/spot/detail_2924.html Mie Prefectural tourism website] *[https://www.tsukanko.jp/spot/18/ Tsu city tourism website] {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]][[en:Yūki Shrine]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Yuki Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:1879 establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Tsu, Mie]] [[Category:Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] [[Category:qq--qq]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 7]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Yūki Shrine}][[Category:qq]] [[en:Yūki Shrine]]]] [[en:Yūki Shrine]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 7]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Yūki Shrine}][[Category:qq]] [[en:Yūki Shrine]]]] [[Category:Bekkaku Kanpeisha]] [[en:Yūki Shrine]][[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 7]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Yūki Shrine}][[Category:qq]] [[en:Yūki Shrine]]]] [[en:Yūki Shrine]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 7]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Yūki Shrine}][[Category:qq]] [[en:Yūki Shrine]]]] [[en:Yūki Shrine]][[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 7]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Yūki Shrine}][[Category:qq]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:Yūki Shrine]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Yūki Shrine]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:Bekkaku-sha]] [[en:Yūki Shrine]]]] [[en:Yūki Shrine]][[en:Yūki Shrine]] {{Shinto-stub}}

Its main festival is held annually on May 1, 2 and 3. It is one of the [[Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]]. ==History== [[Yūki Munehiro]] (1266 – 1339) was a vassal of the [[Kamakura shogunate]] who had been entrusted with southern [[Mutsu Province]] and was castellan of [[Shirakawa Castle]] He was dispatched by [[Hōjō Takatoki]] to [[Kyoto]] in 1331 during the [[Genkō War]], but subsequently defected to the side of [[Emperor Go-Daigo]]. He returned as one of [[Nitta Yoshisada]]'s generals during the 1333 [[Siege of Kamakura (1333)|Battle of Kamakura]] to overthrow the [[Hōjō clan| Hōjō]]. Following the [[Kenmu Restoration]], he served alongside [[Kusunoki Masashige]] as an aide to Emperor Go-Daigo and assisted [[Kitabatake Akiie]] conquer Mutsu Province. When [[Ashikaga Takauji]] rebelled against Emperor Go-Daigo, he led the army which retook Kyoto from the Ashikaga in 1336 and was awarded a sword from the emperor. However, in 1338 Ashikaga Takauji retook Kyoto, and Yūki Munehiro was forced to flee to [[Yoshino, Nara]] where Emperor Go-Daigo had established the [[Southern Court]]. He subsequently crossed into [[Ise Province]] and attempted to take a boat back to Mutsu to rally his forces against the Ashikaga, but was shipwrecked and died.{{Cite book |last=Sansom |first=George |title=A History of Japan, 1334-1615 |publisher=Stanford University Press |year=1961 |ISBN=0804705259 |page=43,48,66}} In 1824, [[Tōdō Takasawa]], the ''[[daimyō]]'' of [[Tsu Domain]] erected a small shrine on the site of his grave. Following the [[Meiji Restoration]] and the establishment of [[State Shinto]], the [[Meiji government]] wished to sponsor patriotism by recalling the events and people of the previous Kenmu Restoration. Yūki Shrine was given the official status of a village shrine in 1879 under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]]. [[Emperor Meiji]] visited Mie Prefecture the following year and made an offering of 200 [[Yen]] to the shrine. The shrine was promoted to special status in 1882 as one of the "Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration". The shrine was burned down in July 1945 during World War II, and was reconstructed in the late 1950s. {{Infobox religious building | name = Yūki Shrine
結城神社 | image = Yuukizinnjya.jpg | caption = ''Heiden'' of Yūki Shrine | map_type = Japan Mie Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34.6977|136.5152|type:landmark_region:JP|format=dms|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Yūki Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] | deity = [[Yūki Munehiro]] | founded_by = | established =1824, restored 1879 | date_destroyed = | location = 2341 Fujikata, Tsu City, Mie Prefecture | website = | architecture_style = | festival = May 1-3 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Yūki Shrine'''|結城神社|Yūki jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the city of [[Tsu, Mie|Tsu]], [[Mie Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. Its main festival is held annually on May 1, 2 and 3. It is one of the [[Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]]. ==History== [[Yūki Munehiro]] (1266 – 1339) was a vassal of the [[Kamakura shogunate]] who had been entrusted with southern [[Mutsu Province]] and was castellan of [[Shirakawa Castle]] He was dispatched by [[Hōjō Takatoki]] to [[Kyoto]] in 1331 during the [[Genkō War]], but subsequently defected to the side of [[Emperor Go-Daigo]]. He returned as one of [[Nitta Yoshisada]]'s generals during the 1333 [[Siege of Kamakura (1333)|Battle of Kamakura]] to overthrow the [[Hōjō clan| Hōjō]]. Following the [[Kenmu Restoration]], he served alongside [[Kusunoki Masashige]] as an aide to Emperor Go-Daigo and assisted [[Kitabatake Akiie]] conquer Mutsu Province. When [[Ashikaga Takauji]] rebelled against Emperor Go-Daigo, he led the army which retook Kyoto from the Ashikaga in 1336 and was awarded a sword from the emperor. However, in 1338 Ashikaga Takauji retook Kyoto, and Yūki Munehiro was forced to flee to [[Yoshino, Nara]] where Emperor Go-Daigo had established the [[Southern Court]]. He subsequently crossed into [[Ise Province]] and attempted to take a boat back to Mutsu to rally his forces against the Ashikaga, but was shipwrecked and died.{{Cite book |last=Sansom |first=George |title=A History of Japan, 1334-1615 |publisher=Stanford University Press |year=1961 |ISBN=0804705259 |page=43,48,66}} In 1824, [[Tōdō Takasawa]], the ''[[daimyō]]'' of [[Tsu Domain]] erected a small shrine on the site of his grave. Following the [[Meiji Restoration]] and the establishment of [[State Shinto]], the [[Meiji government]] wished to sponsor patriotism by recalling the events and people of the previous Kenmu Restoration. Yūki Shrine was given the official status of a village shrine in 1879 under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]]. [[Emperor Meiji]] visited Mie Prefecture the following year and made an offering of 200 [[Yen]] to the shrine. The shrine was promoted to special status in 1882 as one of the "Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration". The shrine was burned down in July 1945 during World War II, and was reconstructed in the late 1950s. It is located approximately one kilometer southeast of [[Akogi Station]] on the [[Kisei Main Line]]. ==See also== *[[Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External The shrine was burned down in July 1945 during World War II, and was reconstructed in the late 1950s. It is located approximately one kilometer southeast of [[Akogi Station]] on the [[Kisei Main Line]]. ==See also== *[[Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External {{Infobox religious building | name = Yūki Shrine
結城神社 | image = Yuukizinnjya.jpg | caption = ''Heiden'' of Yūki Shrine | map_type = Japan Mie Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34.6977|136.5152|type:landmark_region:JP|format=dms|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Yūki Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] | deity = [[Yūki Munehiro]] | founded_by = | established =1824, restored 1879 | date_destroyed = | location = 2341 Fujikata, Tsu City, Mie Prefecture | website = | architecture_style = | festival = May 1-3 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Yūki Shrine'''|結城神社|Yūki jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the city of [[Tsu, Mie|Tsu]], [[Mie Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. Its main festival is held annually on May 1, 2 and 3. It is one of the [[Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]]. ==History== [[Yūki Munehiro]] (1266 – 1339) was a vassal of the [[Kamakura shogunate]] who had been entrusted with southern [[Mutsu Province]] and was castellan of [[Shirakawa Castle]] He was dispatched by [[Hōjō Takatoki]] to [[Kyoto]] in 1331 during the [[Genkō War]], but subsequently defected to the side of [[Emperor Go-Daigo]]. He returned as one of [[Nitta Yoshisada]]'s generals during the 1333 [[Siege of Kamakura (1333)|Battle of Kamakura]] to overthrow the [[Hōjō clan| Hōjō]]. Following the [[Kenmu Restoration]], he served alongside [[Kusunoki Masashige]] as an aide to Emperor Go-Daigo and assisted [[Kitabatake Akiie]] conquer Mutsu Province. When [[Ashikaga Takauji]] rebelled against Emperor Go-Daigo, he led the army which retook Kyoto from the Ashikaga in 1336 and was awarded a sword from the emperor. However, in 1338 Ashikaga Takauji retook Kyoto, and Yūki Munehiro was forced to flee to [[Yoshino, Nara]] where Emperor Go-Daigo had established the [[Southern Court]]. He subsequently crossed into [[Ise Province]] and attempted to take a boat back to Mutsu to rally his forces against the Ashikaga, but was shipwrecked and died.{{Cite book |last=Sansom |first=George |title=A History of Japan, 1334-1615 |publisher=Stanford University Press |year=1961 |ISBN=0804705259 |page=43,48,66}} In 1824, [[Tōdō Takasawa]], the ''[[daimyō]]'' of [[Tsu Domain]] erected a small shrine on the site of his grave. Following the [[Meiji Restoration]] and the establishment of [[State Shinto]], the [[Meiji government]] wished to sponsor patriotism by recalling the events and people of the previous Kenmu Restoration. Yūki Shrine was given the official status of a village shrine in 1879 under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]]. [[Emperor Meiji]] visited Mie Prefecture the following year and made an offering of 200 [[Yen]] to the shrine. The shrine was promoted to special status in 1882 as one of the "Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration". The shrine was burned down in July 1945 during World War II, and was reconstructed in the late 1950s. It is located approximately one kilometer southeast of [[Akogi Station]] on the [[Kisei Main Line]]. ==See also== *[[Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External Following the [[Meiji Restoration]] and the establishment of [[State Shinto]], the [[Meiji government]] wished to sponsor patriotism by recalling the events and people of the previous Kenmu Restoration. Yūki Shrine was given the official status of a village shrine in 1879 under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]]. [[Emperor Meiji]] visited Mie Prefecture the following year and made an offering of 200 [[Yen]] to the shrine. The shrine was promoted to special status in 1882 as one of the "Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration". The shrine was burned down in July 1945 during World War II, and was reconstructed in the late 1950s. It is located approximately one kilometer southeast of [[Akogi Station]] on the [[Kisei Main Line]]. ==See also== *[[Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External Its main festival is held annually on May 1, 2 and 3. It is one of the [[Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]]. ==History== [[Yūki Munehiro]] (1266 – 1339) was a vassal of the [[Kamakura shogunate]] who had been entrusted with southern [[Mutsu Province]] and was castellan of [[Shirakawa Castle]] He was dispatched by [[Hōjō Takatoki]] to [[Kyoto]] in 1331 during the [[Genkō War]], but subsequently defected to the side of [[Emperor Go-Daigo]]. He returned as one of [[Nitta Yoshisada]]'s generals during the 1333 [[Siege of Kamakura (1333)|Battle of Kamakura]] to overthrow the [[Hōjō clan| Hōjō]]. Following the [[Kenmu Restoration]], he served alongside [[Kusunoki Masashige]] as an aide to Emperor Go-Daigo and assisted [[Kitabatake Akiie]] conquer Mutsu Province. When [[Ashikaga Takauji]] rebelled against Emperor Go-Daigo, he led the army which retook Kyoto from the Ashikaga in 1336 and was awarded a sword from the emperor. However, in 1338 Ashikaga Takauji retook Kyoto, and Yūki Munehiro was forced to flee to [[Yoshino, Nara]] where Emperor Go-Daigo had established the [[Southern Court]]. He subsequently crossed into [[Ise Province]] and attempted to take a boat back to Mutsu to rally his forces against the Ashikaga, but was shipwrecked and died.{{Cite book |last=Sansom |first=George |title=A History of Japan, 1334-1615 |publisher=Stanford University Press |year=1961 |ISBN=0804705259 |page=43,48,66}} In 1824, [[Tōdō Takasawa]], the ''[[daimyō]]'' of [[Tsu Domain]] erected a small shrine on the site of his grave. Following the [[Meiji Restoration]] and the establishment of [[State Shinto]], the [[Meiji government]] wished to sponsor patriotism by recalling the events and people of the previous Kenmu Restoration. Yūki Shrine was given the official status of a village shrine in 1879 under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]]. [[Emperor Meiji]] visited Mie Prefecture the following year and made an offering of 200 [[Yen]] to the shrine. The shrine was promoted to special status in 1882 as one of the "Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration". The shrine was burned down in July 1945 during World War II, and was reconstructed in the late 1950s. It is located approximately one kilometer southeast of [[Akogi Station]] on the [[Kisei Main Line]]. ==See also== *[[Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External Its main festival is held annually on May 1, 2 and 3. It is one of the [[Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]]. ==History== [[Yūki Munehiro]] (1266 – 1339) was a vassal of the [[Kamakura shogunate]] who had been entrusted with southern [[Mutsu Province]] and was castellan of [[Shirakawa Castle]] He was dispatched by [[Hōjō Takatoki]] to [[Kyoto]] in 1331 during the [[Genkō War]], but subsequently defected to the side of [[Emperor Go-Daigo]]. He returned as one of [[Nitta Yoshisada]]'s generals during the 1333 [[Siege of Kamakura (1333)|Battle of Kamakura]] to overthrow the [[Hōjō clan| Hōjō]]. Following the [[Kenmu Restoration]], he served alongside [[Kusunoki Masashige]] as an aide to Emperor Go-Daigo and assisted [[Kitabatake Akiie]] conquer Mutsu Province. When [[Ashikaga Takauji]] rebelled against Emperor Go-Daigo, he led the army which retook Kyoto from the Ashikaga in 1336 and was awarded a sword from the emperor. However, in 1338 Ashikaga Takauji retook Kyoto, and Yūki Munehiro was forced to flee to [[Yoshino, Nara]] where Emperor Go-Daigo had established the [[Southern Court]]. He subsequently crossed into [[Ise Province]] and attempted to take a boat back to Mutsu to rally his forces against the Ashikaga, but was shipwrecked and died.{{Cite book |last=Sansom |first=George |title=A History of Japan, 1334-1615 |publisher=Stanford University Press |year=1961 |ISBN=0804705259 |page=43,48,66}} In 1824, [[Tōdō Takasawa]], the ''[[daimyō]]'' of [[Tsu Domain]] erected a small shrine on the site of his grave. Following the [[Meiji Restoration]] and the establishment of [[State Shinto]], the [[Meiji government]] wished to sponsor patriotism by recalling the events and people of the previous Kenmu Restoration. Yūki Shrine was given the official status of a village shrine in 1879 under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]]. [[Emperor Meiji]] visited Mie Prefecture the following year and made an offering of 200 [[Yen]] to the shrine. The shrine was promoted to special status in 1882 as one of the "Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration". The shrine was burned down in July 1945 during World War II, and was reconstructed in the late 1950s. It is located approximately one kilometer southeast of [[Akogi Station]] on the [[Kisei Main Line]]. ==See also== *[[Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building | name = Yūki Shrine
結城神社 | image = Yuukizinnjya.jpg | caption = ''Heiden'' of Yūki Shrine | map_type = Japan Mie Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34.6977|136.5152|type:landmark_region:JP|format=dms|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Yūki Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] | deity = [[Yūki Munehiro]] | founded_by = | established =1824, restored 1879 | date_destroyed = | location = 2341 Fujikata, Tsu City, Mie Prefecture | website = | architecture_style = | festival = May 1-3 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Yūki Shrine'''|結城神社|Yūki jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the city of [[Tsu, Mie|Tsu]], [[Mie Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. Its main festival is held annually on May 1, 2 and 3. It is one of the [[Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]]. ==History== [[Yūki Munehiro]] (1266 – 1339) was a vassal of the [[Kamakura shogunate]] who had been entrusted with southern [[Mutsu Province]] and was castellan of [[Shirakawa Castle]] He was dispatched by [[Hōjō Takatoki]] to [[Kyoto]] in 1331 during the [[Genkō War]], but subsequently defected to the side of [[Emperor Go-Daigo]]. He returned as one of [[Nitta Yoshisada]]'s generals during the 1333 [[Siege of Kamakura (1333)|Battle of Kamakura]] to overthrow the [[Hōjō clan| Hōjō]]. Following the [[Kenmu Restoration]], he served alongside [[Kusunoki Masashige]] as an aide to Emperor Go-Daigo and assisted [[Kitabatake Akiie]] conquer Mutsu Province. When [[Ashikaga Takauji]] rebelled against Emperor Go-Daigo, he led the army which retook Kyoto from the Ashikaga in 1336 and was awarded a sword from the emperor. However, in 1338 Ashikaga Takauji retook Kyoto, and Yūki Munehiro was forced to flee to [[Yoshino, Nara]] where Emperor Go-Daigo had established the [[Southern Court]]. He subsequently crossed into [[Ise Province]] and attempted to take a boat back to Mutsu to rally his forces against the Ashikaga, but was shipwrecked and died.{{Cite book |last=Sansom |first=George |title=A History of Japan, 1334-1615 |publisher=Stanford University Press |year=1961 |ISBN=0804705259 |page=43,48,66}} In 1824, [[Tōdō Takasawa]], the ''[[daimyō]]'' of [[Tsu Domain]] erected a small shrine on the site of his grave. Following the [[Meiji Restoration]] and the establishment of [[State Shinto]], the [[Meiji government]] wished to sponsor patriotism by recalling the events and people of the previous Kenmu Restoration. Yūki Shrine was given the official status of a village shrine in 1879 under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]]. [[Emperor Meiji]] visited Mie Prefecture the following year and made an offering of 200 [[Yen]] to the shrine. The shrine was promoted to special status in 1882 as one of the "Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration". The shrine was burned down in July 1945 during World War II, and was reconstructed in the late 1950s. It is located approximately one kilometer southeast of [[Akogi Station]] on the [[Kisei Main Line]]. ==See also== *[[Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== *[http://www.kankomie.or.jp/spot/detail_2924.html Mie Prefectural tourism website] *[https://www.tsukanko.jp/spot/18/ Tsu city tourism website] {{Shinto shrine}} ]] ]] ]] ]] ]] {{Shinto-stub}} {{Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:1879 establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Tsu, Mie]] [[Category:Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] [[Category:Completed pages from Wikipedia]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 7]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:11 7]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Bekkaku Kanpeisha]] [[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Yūki Shrine]] [[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:Bekkaku-sha]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Expanded pages from wikipedia]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] }} [[en:Yūki Shrine]] [[sn:Yūki Shrine]] [[ja:Yūki Shrine]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Yuki Shrine}}

Its main festival is held annually on May 1, 2 and 3. It is one of the [[Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]]. ==History== [[Yūki Munehiro]] (1266 – 1339) was a vassal of the [[Kamakura shogunate]] who had been entrusted with southern [[Mutsu Province]] and was castellan of [[Shirakawa Castle]] He was dispatched by [[Hōjō Takatoki]] to [[Kyoto]] in 1331 during the [[Genkō War]], but subsequently defected to the side of [[Emperor Go-Daigo]]. He returned as one of [[Nitta Yoshisada]]'s generals during the 1333 [[Siege of Kamakura (1333)|Battle of Kamakura]] to overthrow the [[Hōjō clan| Hōjō]]. Following the [[Kenmu Restoration]], he served alongside [[Kusunoki Masashige]] as an aide to Emperor Go-Daigo and assisted [[Kitabatake Akiie]] conquer Mutsu Province. When [[Ashikaga Takauji]] rebelled against Emperor Go-Daigo, he led the army which retook Kyoto from the Ashikaga in 1336 and was awarded a sword from the emperor. However, in 1338 Ashikaga Takauji retook Kyoto, and Yūki Munehiro was forced to flee to [[Yoshino, Nara]] where Emperor Go-Daigo had established the [[Southern Court]]. He subsequently crossed into [[Ise Province]] and attempted to take a boat back to Mutsu to rally his forces against the Ashikaga, but was shipwrecked and died.{{Cite book |last=Sansom |first=George |title=A History of Japan, 1334-1615 |publisher=Stanford University Press |year=1961 |ISBN=0804705259 |page=43,48,66}} In 1824, [[Tōdō Takasawa]], the ''[[daimyō]]'' of [[Tsu Domain]] erected a small shrine on the site of his grave. Following the [[Meiji Restoration]] and the establishment of [[State Shinto]], the [[Meiji government]] wished to sponsor patriotism by recalling the events and people of the previous Kenmu Restoration. Yūki Shrine was given the official status of a village shrine in 1879 under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]]. [[Emperor Meiji]] visited Mie Prefecture the following year and made an offering of 200 [[Yen]] to the shrine. The shrine was promoted to special status in 1882 as one of the "Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration". The shrine was burned down in July 1945 during World War II, and was reconstructed in the late 1950s. It is located approximately one kilometer southeast of [[Akogi Station]] on the [[Kisei Main Line]]. ==See also== *[[Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== *[http://www.kankomie.or.jp/spot/detail_2924.html Mie Prefectural tourism website] *[https://www.tsukanko.jp/spot/18/ Tsu city tourism website] {{Shinto shrine}}]]]] {{Shinto-stub}} {{Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:1879 establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Tsu, Mie]] [[Category:Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] [[Category:Completed pages from Wikipedia]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 7]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Bekkaku Kanpeisha]] [[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Yūki Shrine]] [[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:Bekkaku-sha]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Expanded pages from wikipedia]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Yūki Shrine]][[Category:qq]] }} [[en:Yūki Shrine]] [[sn:Yūki Shrine]] [[ja:Yūki Shrine]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Yuki Shrine}}

{{Infobox religious building | name = Yūki Shrine
結城神社 | image = Yuukizinnjya.jpg | caption = ''Heiden'' of Yūki Shrine | map_type = Japan Mie Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34.6977|136.5152|type:landmark_region:JP|format=dms|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Yūki Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] | deity = [[Yūki Munehiro]] | founded_by = | established =1824, restored 1879 | date_destroyed = | location = 2341 Fujikata, Tsu City, Mie Prefecture | website = | architecture_style = | festival = May 1-3 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Yūki Shrine'''|結城神社|Yūki jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the city of [[Tsu, Mie|Tsu]], [[Mie Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. Its main festival is held annually on May 1, 2 and 3. It is one of the [[Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]]. ==History== [[Yūki Munehiro]] (1266 – 1339) was a vassal of the [[Kamakura shogunate]] who had been entrusted with southern [[Mutsu Province]] and was castellan of [[Shirakawa Castle]] He was dispatched by [[Hōjō Takatoki]] to [[Kyoto]] in 1331 during the [[Genkō War]], but subsequently defected to the side of [[Emperor Go-Daigo]]. He returned as one of [[Nitta Yoshisada]]'s generals during the 1333 [[Siege of Kamakura (1333)|Battle of Kamakura]] to overthrow the [[Hōjō clan| Hōjō]]. Following the [[Kenmu Restoration]], he served alongside [[Kusunoki Masashige]] as an aide to Emperor Go-Daigo and assisted [[Kitabatake Akiie]] conquer Mutsu Province. When [[Ashikaga Takauji]] rebelled against Emperor Go-Daigo, he led the army which retook Kyoto from the Ashikaga in 1336 and was awarded a sword from the emperor. However, in 1338 Ashikaga Takauji retook Kyoto, and Yūki Munehiro was forced to flee to [[Yoshino, Nara]] where Emperor Go-Daigo had established the [[Southern Court]]. He subsequently crossed into [[Ise Province]] and attempted to take a boat back to Mutsu to rally his forces against the Ashikaga, but was shipwrecked and died.{{Cite book |last=Sansom |first=George |title=A History of Japan, 1334-1615 |publisher=Stanford University Press |year=1961 |ISBN=0804705259 |page=43,48,66}} In 1824, [[Tōdō Takasawa]], the ''[[daimyō]]'' of [[Tsu Domain]] erected a small shrine on the site of his grave. Following the [[Meiji Restoration]] and the establishment of [[State Shinto]], the [[Meiji government]] wished to sponsor patriotism by recalling the events and people of the previous Kenmu Restoration. Yūki Shrine was given the official status of a village shrine in 1879 under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]]. [[Emperor Meiji]] visited Mie Prefecture the following year and made an offering of 200 [[Yen]] to the shrine. The shrine was promoted to special status in 1882 as one of the "Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration". The shrine was burned down in July 1945 during World War II, and was reconstructed in the late 1950s. It is located approximately one kilometer southeast of [[Akogi Station]] on the [[Kisei Main Line]]. ==See also== *[[Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== *[http://www.kankomie.or.jp/spot/detail_2924.html Mie Prefectural tourism website] *[https://www.tsukanko.jp/spot/18/ Tsu city tourism website] {{Shinto shrine}}]]]] {{Shinto-stub}} {{Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration}} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:1879 establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Tsu, Mie]] [[Category:Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] [[Category:Completed pages from Wikipedia]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Yūki Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 7]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Bekkaku Kanpeisha]] [[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Yūki Shrine]] [[Category:Bekkaku-sha]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Expanded pages from wikipedia]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] }} [[sn:Yūki Shrine]] [[en:Yūki Shrine]] [[ja:Yūki Shrine]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Yuki Shrine}}

Its main festival is held annually on May 1, 2 and 3. It is one of the [[Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]]. ==History== [[Yūki Munehiro]] (1266 – 1339) was a vassal of the [[Kamakura shogunate]] who had been entrusted with southern [[Mutsu Province]] and was castellan of [[Shirakawa Castle]] He was dispatched by [[Hōjō Takatoki]] to [[Kyoto]] in 1331 during the [[Genkō War]], but subsequently defected to the side of [[Emperor Go-Daigo]]. He returned as one of [[Nitta Yoshisada]]'s generals during the 1333 [[Siege of Kamakura (1333)|Battle of Kamakura]] to overthrow the [[Hōjō clan| Hōjō]]. Following the [[Kenmu Restoration]], he served alongside [[Kusunoki Masashige]] as an aide to Emperor Go-Daigo and assisted [[Kitabatake Akiie]] conquer Mutsu Province. When [[Ashikaga Takauji]] rebelled against Emperor Go-Daigo, he led the army which retook Kyoto from the Ashikaga in 1336 and was awarded a sword from the emperor. However, in 1338 Ashikaga Takauji retook Kyoto, and Yūki Munehiro was forced to flee to [[Yoshino, Nara]] where Emperor Go-Daigo had established the [[Southern Court]]. He subsequently crossed into [[Ise Province]] and attempted to take a boat back to Mutsu to rally his forces against the Ashikaga, but was shipwrecked and died.{{Cite book |last=Sansom |first=George |title=A History of Japan, 1334-1615 |publisher=Stanford University Press |year=1961 |ISBN=0804705259 |page=43,48,66}} In 1824, [[Tōdō Takasawa]], the ''[[daimyō]]'' of [[Tsu Domain]] erected a small shrine on the site of his grave. Following the [[Meiji Restoration]] and the establishment of [[State Shinto]], the [[Meiji government]] wished to sponsor patriotism by recalling the events and people of the previous Kenmu Restoration. Yūki Shrine was given the official status of a village shrine in 1879 under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]]. [[Emperor Meiji]] visited Mie Prefecture the following year and made an offering of 200 [[Yen]] to the shrine. The shrine was promoted to special status in 1882 as one of the "Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration". The shrine was burned down in July 1945 during World War II, and was reconstructed in the late 1950s. It is located approximately one kilometer southeast of [[Akogi Station]] on the [[Kisei Main Line]]. ==See also== *[[Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== *[http://www.kankomie.or.jp/spot/detail_2924.html Mie Prefectural tourism website] *[https://www.tsukanko.jp/spot/18/ Tsu city tourism website] {{Shinto shrine}}]]]] {{Shinto-stub}} {{Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:1879 establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Tsu, Mie]] [[Category:Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] [[Category:Completed pages from Wikipedia]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Yūki Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 7]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Bekkaku Kanpeisha]] [[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Yūki Shrine]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Expanded pages from wikipedia]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Yūki Shrine]][[Category:qq]] }} [[sn:Yūki Shrine]] [[en:Yūki Shrine]] [[sn:ūki Shrine]] [[sn: failed]]

{{Infobox religious building | name = Yūki Shrine
結城神社 | image = Yuukizinnjya.jpg | caption = ''Heiden'' of Yūki Shrine | map_type = Japan Mie Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34.6977|136.5152|type:landmark_region:JP|format=dms|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Yūki Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] | deity = [[Yūki Munehiro]] | founded_by = | established =1824, restored 1879 | date_destroyed = | location = 2341 Fujikata, Tsu City, Mie Prefecture | website = | architecture_style = | festival = May 1-3 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Yūki Shrine'''|結城神社|Yūki jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the city of [[Tsu, Mie|Tsu]], [[Mie Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. Its main festival is held annually on May 1, 2 and 3. It is one of the [[Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]]. ==History== [[Yūki Munehiro]] (1266 – 1339) was a vassal of the [[Kamakura shogunate]] who had been entrusted with southern [[Mutsu Province]] and was castellan of [[Shirakawa Castle]] He was dispatched by [[Hōjō Takatoki]] to [[Kyoto]] in 1331 during the [[Genkō War]], but subsequently defected to the side of [[Emperor Go-Daigo]]. He returned as one of [[Nitta Yoshisada]]'s generals during the 1333 [[Siege of Kamakura (1333)|Battle of Kamakura]] to overthrow the [[Hōjō clan| Hōjō]]. Following the [[Kenmu Restoration]], he served alongside [[Kusunoki Masashige]] as an aide to Emperor Go-Daigo and assisted [[Kitabatake Akiie]] conquer Mutsu Province. When [[Ashikaga Takauji]] rebelled against Emperor Go-Daigo, he led the army which retook Kyoto from the Ashikaga in 1336 and was awarded a sword from the emperor. However, in 1338 Ashikaga Takauji retook Kyoto, and Yūki Munehiro was forced to flee to [[Yoshino, Nara]] where Emperor Go-Daigo had established the [[Southern Court]]. He subsequently crossed into [[Ise Province]] and attempted to take a boat back to Mutsu to rally his forces against the Ashikaga, but was shipwrecked and died.{{Cite book |last=Sansom |first=George |title=A History of Japan, 1334-1615 |publisher=Stanford University Press |year=1961 |ISBN=0804705259 |page=43,48,66}} In 1824, [[Tōdō Takasawa]], the ''[[daimyō]]'' of [[Tsu Domain]] erected a small shrine on the site of his grave. Following the [[Meiji Restoration]] and the establishment of [[State Shinto]], the [[Meiji government]] wished to sponsor patriotism by recalling the events and people of the previous Kenmu Restoration. Yūki Shrine was given the official status of a village shrine in 1879 under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]]. [[Emperor Meiji]] visited Mie Prefecture the following year and made an offering of 200 [[Yen]] to the shrine. The shrine was promoted to special status in 1882 as one of the "Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration". The shrine was burned down in July 1945 during World War II, and was reconstructed in the late 1950s. It is located approximately one kilometer southeast of [[Akogi Station]] on the [[Kisei Main Line]]. ==See also== *[[Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== *[http://www.kankomie.or.jp/spot/detail_2924.html Mie Prefectural tourism website] *[https://www.tsukanko.jp/spot/18/ Tsu city tourism website] {{Shinto shrine}}]]]] {{Shinto-stub}} {{Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:1879 establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Tsu, Mie]] [[Category:Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] [[Category:Completed pages from Wikipedia]] [[sn:Yūki Shrine]][[Category:Pages goodbye $1 Wikipedia 7]] [[Category:Bekkaku Kanpeisha]] [[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Yūki Shrine]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Expanded pages from Wikipedia]] [[Category:completion tier 2]] [[Category:Pages goodbye $1 Wikipedia]] [[sn:Yūki Shrine]]}} [[sn:Yūki Shrine]] [[en:Yūki Shrine]] [[sn:ūki Shrine]] [[sn: failed]]

The shrine was burned down in July 1945 during World War II, and was reconstructed in the late 1950s. It is located approximately one kilometer southeast of [[Akogi Station]] on the [[Kisei Main Line]]. ==See also== *[[Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== *[http://www.kankomie.or.jp/spot/detail_2924.html Mie Prefectural tourism website] *[https://www.tsukanko.jp/spot/18/ Tsu city tourism website] {{Shinto shrine}}]]]] {{Shinto-stub}} {{Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:1879 establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Tsu, Mie]] [[Category:Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] [[Category:Completed pages from Wikipedia]] [[sn:Yūki Shrine]][[Category:Pages goodbye $1 Wikipedia 7]] [[Category:Bekkaku Kanpeisha]] [[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Yūki Shrine]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Expanded pages from Wikipedia]] [[Category:c]][[Completion tier 2]][[Category:qq 2]] [[Category:Pages goodbye $1 Wikipedia]] [[sn:Yūki Shrine]]}} [[sn:Yūki Shrine]] [[en:Yūki Shrine]] [[sn:ūki Shrine]] [[sn: failed]]

Following the [[Kenmu Restoration]], he served alongside [[Kusunoki Masashige]] as an aide to Emperor Go-Daigo and assisted [[Kitabatake Akiie]] conquer Mutsu Province. When [[Ashikaga Takauji]] rebelled against Emperor Go-Daigo, he led the army which retook Kyoto from the Ashikaga in 1336 and was awarded a sword from the emperor. However, in 1338 Ashikaga Takauji retook Kyoto, and Yūki Munehiro was forced to flee to [[Yoshino, Nara]] where Emperor Go-Daigo had established the [[Southern Court]]. He subsequently crossed into [[Ise Province]] and attempted to take a boat back to Mutsu to rally his forces against the Ashikaga, but was shipwrecked and died.{{Cite book |last=Sansom |first=George |title=A History of Japan, 1334-1615 |publisher=Stanford University Press |year=1961 |ISBN=0804705259 |page=43,48,66}} In 1824, [[Tōdō Takasawa]], the ''[[daimyō]]'' of [[Tsu Domain]] erected a small shrine on the site of his grave. Following the [[Meiji Restoration]] and the establishment of [[State Shinto]], the [[Meiji government]] wished to sponsor patriotism by recalling the events and people of the previous Kenmu Restoration. Yūki Shrine was given the official status of a village shrine in 1879 under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]]. [[Emperor Meiji]] visited Mie Prefecture the following year and made an offering of 200 [[Yen]] to the shrine. The shrine was promoted to special status in 1882 as one of the "Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration". The shrine was burned down in July 1945 during World War II, and was reconstructed in the late 1950s. It is located approximately one kilometer southeast of [[Akogi Station]] on the [[Kisei Main Line]]. ==See also== *[[List of the Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration|Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== *[http://www.kankomie.or.jp/spot/detail_2924.html Mie Prefectural tourism website] *[https://www.tsukanko.jp/spot/18/ Tsu city tourism website] {{Shinto shrine}}]]]] {{Shinto-stub}} {{Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:1879 establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Tsu, Mie]] [[Category:Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] [[sn:Yūki Shrine]][[Category:Pages goodbye $1 Wikipedia 7]] [[Category:Bekkaku Kanpeisha]] [[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]][[Category:Pages goodbye $1 Wikipedia]] [[sn:Yūki Shrine]]}} [[sn:Yūki Shrine]] [[en:Yūki Shrine]] [[sn:ūki Shrine]] [[sn: failed]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[fr:Yūki-jinja]] [[ja:結城神社]] [[Category:pages with valid en interwikis]] [[Category:Expanded Pages from Wikipedia]]

[[Emperor Meiji]] visited Mie Prefecture the following year and made an offering of 200 [[Yen]] to the shrine. The shrine was promoted to special status in 1882 as one of the "Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration". 結城神社 | image = Yuukizinnjya.jpg | caption = ''Heiden'' of Yūki Shrine | map_type = Japan Mie Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34.6977|136.5152|type:landmark_region:JP|format=dms|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Yūki Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[List of the Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration|Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] | deity = [[Yūki Munehiro]] | founded_by = | established =1824, restored 1879 | date_destroyed = | location = 2341 Fujikata, Tsu City, Mie Prefecture | website = | architecture_style = | festival = May 1-3 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Yūki Shrine'''|結城神社|Yūki jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the city of [[Tsu, Mie|Tsu]], [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. Its main festival is held annually on May 1, 2 and 3. It is one of the [[List of the Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration|Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]]. ==History== [[Yūki Munehiro]] (1266 – 1339) was a vassal of the [[:en:Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura shogunate]] who had been entrusted with southern [[:en:Mutsu Province|Mutsu Province]] and was castellan of [[Shirakawa Castle]] He was dispatched by [[:en:Hōjō Takatoki|Hōjō Takatoki]] to [[Kyoto]] in 1331 during the [[:en:Genkō War|Genkō War]], but subsequently defected to the side of [[:en:Emperor Go-Daigo|Emperor Go-Daigo]]. He returned as one of [[Nitta Yoshisada]]'s generals during the 1333 [[Siege of Kamakura (1333)|Battle of Kamakura]] to overthrow the [[:en:Hōjō clan| Hōjō]]. Following the [[Kenmu Restoration]], he served alongside [[Kusunoki Masashige]] as an aide to Emperor Go-Daigo and assisted [[Kitabatake Akiie]] conquer Mutsu Province. When [[:en:Ashikaga Takauji|Ashikaga Takauji]] rebelled against Emperor Go-Daigo, he led the army which retook Kyoto from the Ashikaga in 1336 and was awarded a sword from the emperor. However, in 1338 Ashikaga Takauji retook Kyoto, and Yūki Munehiro was forced to flee to [[Yoshino, Nara]] where Emperor Go-Daigo had established the [[Southern Court]]. He subsequently crossed into [[:en:Ise Province|Ise Province]] and attempted to take a boat back to Mutsu to rally his forces against the Ashikaga, but was shipwrecked and died.{{Cite book |last=Sansom |first=George |title=A History of Japan, 1334-1615 |publisher=Stanford University Press |year=1961 |ISBN=0804705259 |page=43,48,66}} In 1824, [[Tōdō Takasawa]], the ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of [[Tsu Domain]] erected a small shrine on the site of his grave. Following the [[Meiji Restoration]] and the establishment of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]], the [[Meiji government]] wished to sponsor patriotism by recalling the events and people of the previous Kenmu Restoration. Yūki Shrine was given the official status of a village shrine in 1879 under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]]. [[:en:Emperor Meiji|Emperor Meiji]] visited Mie Prefecture the following year and made an offering of 200 [[Yen]] to the shrine. The shrine was promoted to special status in 1882 as one of the "Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration". The shrine was burned down in July 1945 during World War II, and was reconstructed in the late 1950s. It is located approximately one kilometer southeast of [[:en:Akogi Station|Akogi Station]] on the [[Kisei Main Line]]. ==See also== *[[List of the Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration|Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== *[http://www.kankomie.or.jp/spot/detail_2924.html Mie Prefectural tourism website] *[https://www.tsukanko.jp/spot/18/ Tsu city tourism website] {{Shinto shrine}}]]]] {{Shinto-stub}} {{Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:1879 establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Tsu, Mie]] [[Category:Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] [[sn:Yūki Shrine]] [[Category:Bekkaku Kanpeisha]] [[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[sn:Yūki Shrine]]}} [[sn:Yūki Shrine]] [[en:Yūki Shrine]] [[sn:ūki Shrine]] [[sn: failed]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[fr:Yūki-jinja]] [[ja:結城神社]] [[Category:Pages with valid en interwikis]] [[Category:Pages with valid en interwikis]] [[Category:Expanded Pages from Wikipedia]]

The shrine was promoted to special status in 1882 as one of the "Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration". The shrine was burned down in July 1945 during World War II, and was reconstructed in the late 1950s. It is located approximately one kilometer southeast of [[:en:Akogi Station|Akogi Station]] on the [[:en:Kisei Main Line|Kisei Main Line]]. ==See also== *[[List of the Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration|Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== *[http://www.kankomie.or.jp/spot/detail_2924.html Mie Prefectural tourism website] *[https://www.tsukanko.jp/spot/18/ Tsu city tourism website] {{Shinto shrine}}]]]] {{Shinto-stub}} {{Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:1879 establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Tsu, Mie]] [[Category:Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] [[sn:Yūki Shrine]] [[Category:Bekkaku Kanpeisha]] [[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[sn:Yūki Shrine]]}} [[sn:Yūki Shrine]] [[en:Yūki Shrine]] [[sn:ūki Shrine]] [[sn: failed]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[fr:Yūki-jinja]] [[ja:結城神社]] [[Category:Pages with valid en interwikis]] [[Category:Pages with valid en interwikis]] [[Category:Expanded Pages from Wikipedia]]

It is one of the [[List of the Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration|Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]]. ==History== [[:en:Yūki Munehiro|Yūki Munehiro]] (1266 – 1339) was a vassal of the [[:en:Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura shogunate]] who had been entrusted with southern [[:en:Mutsu Province|Mutsu Province]] and was castellan of [[:en:Shirakawa Castle|Shirakawa Castle]] He was dispatched by [[:en:Hōjō Takatoki|Hōjō Takatoki]] to [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]] in 1331 during the [[:en:Genkō War|Genkō War]], but subsequently defected to the side of [[:en:Emperor Go-Daigo|Emperor Go-Daigo]]. He returned as one of [[Nitta Yoshisada]]'s generals during the 1333 [[Siege of Kamakura (1333)|Battle of Kamakura]] to overthrow the [[:en:Hōjō clan| Hōjō]]. Following the [[Kenmu Restoration]], he served alongside [[Kusunoki Masashige]] as an aide to Emperor Go-Daigo and assisted [[:en:Kitabatake Akiie|Kitabatake Akiie]] conquer Mutsu Province. When [[:en:Ashikaga Takauji|Ashikaga Takauji]] rebelled against Emperor Go-Daigo, he led the army which retook Kyoto from the Ashikaga in 1336 and was awarded a sword from the emperor. However, in 1338 Ashikaga Takauji retook Kyoto, and Yūki Munehiro was forced to flee to [[:en:Yoshino, Nara|Yoshino, Nara]] where Emperor Go-Daigo had established the [[Southern Court]]. He subsequently crossed into [[:en:Ise Province|Ise Province]] and attempted to take a boat back to Mutsu to rally his forces against the Ashikaga, but was shipwrecked and died.{{Cite book |last=Sansom |first=George |title=A History of Japan, 1334-1615 |publisher=Stanford University Press |year=1961 |ISBN=0804705259 |page=43,48,66}} In 1824, [[Tōdō Takasawa]], the ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of [[Tsu Domain]] erected a small shrine on the site of his grave. Following the [[Meiji Restoration]] and the establishment of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]], the [[:en:Government of Meiji Japan|Government of Meiji Japan]] wished to sponsor patriotism by recalling the events and people of the previous Kenmu Restoration. Yūki Shrine was given the official status of a village shrine in 1879 under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]]. [[:en:Emperor Meiji|Emperor Meiji]] visited Mie Prefecture the following year and made an offering of 200 [[:en:Yen|Yen]] to the shrine. The shrine was promoted to special status in 1882 as one of the "Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration". The shrine was burned down in July 1945 during World War II, and was reconstructed in the late 1950s. It is located approximately one kilometer southeast of [[:en:Akogi Station|Akogi Station]] on the [[:en:Kisei Main Line|Kisei Main Line]]. ==See also== *[[List of the Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration|Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== *[http://www.kankomie.or.jp/spot/detail_2924.html Mie Prefectural tourism website] *[https://www.tsukanko.jp/spot/18/ Tsu city tourism website] {{Shinto shrine}}]]]] {{Shinto-stub}} {{Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:1879 establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Tsu, Mie]] [[Category:Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] [[sn:Yūki Shrine]] [[Category:Bekkaku Kanpeisha]] [[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[sn:Yūki Shrine]]}} [[sn:Yūki Shrine]] [[en:Yūki Shrine]] [[sn:ūki Shrine]] [[sn: failed]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[fr:Yūki-jinja]] [[ja:結城神社]]

When [[:en:Ashikaga Takauji|Ashikaga Takauji]] rebelled against Emperor Go-Daigo, he led the army which retook Kyoto from the Ashikaga in 1336 and was awarded a sword from the emperor. However, in 1338 Ashikaga Takauji retook Kyoto, and Yūki Munehiro was forced to flee to [[:en:Yoshino, Nara|Yoshino, Nara]] where Emperor Go-Daigo had established the [[Southern Court]]. He subsequently crossed into [[:en:Ise Province|Ise Province]] and attempted to take a boat back to Mutsu to rally his forces against the Ashikaga, but was shipwrecked and died.{{Cite book |last=Sansom |first=George |title=A History of Japan, 1334-1615 |publisher=Stanford University Press |year=1961 |ISBN=0804705259 |page=43,48,66}} In 1824, [[:en:Tōdō Takasawa|Tōdō Takasawa]], the ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of [[:en:Tsu Domain|Tsu Domain]] erected a small shrine on the site of his grave. Following the [[Meiji Restoration]] and the establishment of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]], the [[:en:Government of Meiji Japan|Government of Meiji Japan]] wished to sponsor patriotism by recalling the events and people of the previous Kenmu Restoration. Yūki Shrine was given the official status of a village shrine in 1879 under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]]. [[:en:Emperor Meiji|Emperor Meiji]] visited Mie Prefecture the following year and made an offering of 200 [[:en:Yen|Yen]] to the shrine. The shrine was promoted to special status in 1882 as one of the "Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration". The shrine was burned down in July 1945 during World War II, and was reconstructed in the late 1950s. It is located approximately one kilometer southeast of [[:en:Akogi Station|Akogi Station]] on the [[:en:Kisei Main Line|Kisei Main Line]]. ==See also== *[[List of the Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration|Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== *[http://www.kankomie.or.jp/spot/detail_2924.html Mie Prefectural tourism website] *[https://www.tsukanko.jp/spot/18/ Tsu city tourism website] {{Shinto shrine}}]]]] {{Shinto-stub}} {{Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:1879 establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Tsu, Mie]] [[Category:Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] [[sn:Yūki Shrine]] [[Category:Bekkaku Kanpeisha]] [[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[sn:Yūki Shrine]]}} [[sn:Yūki Shrine]] [[en:Yūki Shrine]] [[sn:ūki Shrine]] [[sn: failed]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[fr:Yūki-jinja]] [[ja:結城神社]]

Following the [[Kenmu Restoration]], he served alongside [[Kusunoki Masashige]] as an aide to Emperor Go-Daigo and assisted [[:en:Kitabatake Akiie|Kitabatake Akiie]] conquer Mutsu Province. When [[:en:Ashikaga Takauji|Ashikaga Takauji]] rebelled against Emperor Go-Daigo, he led the army which retook Kyoto from the Ashikaga in 1336 and was awarded a sword from the emperor. However, in 1338 Ashikaga Takauji retook Kyoto, and Yūki Munehiro was forced to flee to [[:en:Yoshino, Nara|Yoshino, Nara]] where Emperor Go-Daigo had established the [[Southern Court]]. He subsequently crossed into [[:en:Ise Province|Ise Province]] and attempted to take a boat back to Mutsu to rally his forces against the Ashikaga, but was shipwrecked and died.{{Cite book |last=Sansom |first=George |title=A History of Japan, 1334-1615 |publisher=Stanford University Press |year=1961 |ISBN=0804705259 |page=43,48,66}} In 1824, [[:en:Tōdō Takasawa|Tōdō Takasawa]], the ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of [[:en:Tsu Domain|Tsu Domain]] erected a small shrine on the site of his grave. The shrine was burned down in July 1945 during World War II, and was reconstructed in the late 1950s. It is located approximately one kilometer southeast of [[:en:Akogi Station|Akogi Station]] on the [[:en:Kisei Main Line|Kisei Main Line]]. ==See also== *[[List of the Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration|Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== *[http://www.kankomie.or.jp/spot/detail_2924.html Mie Prefectural tourism website] *[https://www.tsukanko.jp/spot/18/ Tsu city tourism website] ]]]] {{Shinto-stub}} {{Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:1879 establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Tsu, Mie]] [[Category:Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] [[sn:Yūki Shrine]] [[Category:Bekkaku Kanpeisha]] [[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[sn:Yūki Shrine]]}} [[sn:Yūki Shrine]] [[en:Yūki Shrine]] [[sn:ūki Shrine]] [[sn: failed]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[fr:Yūki-jinja]] [[ja:結城神社]]

However, in 1338 Ashikaga Takauji retook Kyoto, and Yūki Munehiro was forced to flee to [[:en:Yoshino, Nara|Yoshino, Nara]] where Emperor Go-Daigo had established the [[Southern Court]]. He subsequently crossed into [[:en:Ise Province|Ise Province]] and attempted to take a boat back to Mutsu to rally his forces against the Ashikaga, but was shipwrecked and died.{{Cite book |last=Sansom |first=George |title=A History of Japan, 1334-1615 |publisher=Stanford University Press |year=1961 |ISBN=0804705259 |page=43,48,66}} In 1824, [[:en:Tōdō Takasawa|Tōdō Takasawa]], the ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of [[:en:Tsu Domain|Tsu Domain]] erected a small shrine on the site of his grave. Following the [[Meiji Restoration]] and the establishment of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]], the [[:en:Government of Meiji Japan|Government of Meiji Japan]] wished to sponsor patriotism by recalling the events and people of the previous Kenmu Restoration. Yūki Shrine was given the official status of a village shrine in 1879 under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]]. [[:en:Emperor Meiji|Emperor Meiji]] visited Mie Prefecture the following year and made an offering of 200 [[:en:Yen|Yen]] to the shrine. The shrine was promoted to special status in 1882 as one of the "Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration". The shrine was burned down in July 1945 during World War II, and was reconstructed in the late 1950s. It is located approximately one kilometer southeast of [[:en:Akogi Station|Akogi Station]] on the [[:en:Kisei Main Line|Kisei Main Line]]. ==See also== *[[List of the Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration|Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== *[http://www.kankomie.or.jp/spot/detail_2924.html Mie Prefectural tourism website] *[https://www.tsukanko.jp/spot/18/ Tsu city tourism website] ]]]] {{Shinto-stub}} {{Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:1879 establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Tsu, Mie]] [[Category:Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] [[sn:Yūki Shrine]] [[Category:Bekkaku Kanpeisha]] [[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[sn:Yūki Shrine]]}} [[sn:Yūki Shrine]] [[en:Yūki Shrine]] [[sn:ūki Shrine]] [[sn: failed]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[en:Yūki Shrine]] [[fr:Yūki-jinja]] [[ja:結城神社]]
