Its main festival is held annually on May 1, 2 and 3. It is one of the [[List of the Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration|Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]]. ==History== [[:en:Yūki Munehiro|Yūki Munehiro]] (1266 – 1339) was a vassal of the [[:en:Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura shogunate]] who had been entrusted with southern [[:en:Mutsu Province|Mutsu Province]] and was castellan of [[:en:Shirakawa Castle|Shirakawa Castle]] He was dispatched by [[:en:Hōjō Takatoki|Hōjō Takatoki]] to [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]] in 1331 during the [[:en:Genkō War|Genkō War]], but subsequently defected to the side of [[:en:Emperor Go-Daigo|Emperor Go-Daigo]]. He returned as one of [[Nitta Yoshisada]]'s generals during the 1333 [[:en:Siege of Kamakura (1333)|Battle of Kamakura]] to overthrow the [[:en:Hōjō clan| Hōjō]]. Following the [[Kenmu Restoration]], he served alongside [[Kusunoki Masashige]] as an aide to Emperor Go-Daigo and assisted [[:en:Kitabatake Akiie|Kitabatake Akiie]] conquer Mutsu Province. When [[:en:Ashikaga Takauji|Ashikaga Takauji]] rebelled against Emperor Go-Daigo, he led the army which retook Kyoto from the Ashikaga in 1336 and was awarded a sword from the emperor. However, in 1338 Ashikaga Takauji retook Kyoto, and Yūki Munehiro was forced to flee to [[:en:Yoshino, Nara|Yoshino, Nara]] where Emperor Go-Daigo had established the [[Southern Court]]. He subsequently crossed into [[:en:Ise Province|Ise Province]] and attempted to take a boat back to Mutsu to rally his forces against the Ashikaga, but was shipwrecked and died.{{Cite book |last=Sansom |first=George |title=A History of Japan, 1334-1615 |publisher=Stanford University Press |year=1961 |ISBN=0804705259 |page=43,48,66}} In 1824, [[:en:Tōdō Takasawa|Tōdō Takasawa]], the ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of [[:en:Tsu Domain|Tsu Domain]] erected a small shrine on the site of his grave. Following the [[Meiji Restoration]] and the establishment of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]], the [[:en:Government of Meiji Japan|Government of Meiji Japan]] wished to sponsor patriotism by recalling the events and people of the previous Kenmu Restoration. Yūki Shrine was given the official status of a village shrine in 1879 under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]]. [[:en:Emperor Meiji|Emperor Meiji]] visited Mie Prefecture the following year and made an offering of 200 [[:en:Yen|Yen]] to the shrine. The shrine was promoted to special status in 1882 as one of the "Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration". The shrine was burned down in July 1945 during World War II, and was reconstructed in the late 1950s. It is located approximately one kilometer southeast of [[:en:Akogi Station|Akogi Station]] on the [[:en:Kisei Main Line|Kisei Main Line]]. ==See also== *[[List of the Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration|Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== *[http://www.kankomie.or.jp/spot/detail_2924.html Mie Prefectural tourism website] *[https://www.tsukanko.jp/spot/18/ Tsu city tourism website] ]]]] {{Shinto-stub}} {{Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:1879 establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Tsu, Mie]] [[Category:Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] [[sn:Yūki Shrine]] [[Category:Bekkaku Kanpeisha]] [[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[sn:Yūki Shrine]]}} [[sn:Yūki Shrine]] [[en:Yūki Shrine]] [[sn:ūki Shrine]] [[sn: failed]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[en:Yūki Shrine]] [[fr:Yūki-jinja]] [[ja:結城神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11606619}}

He subsequently crossed into [[:en:Ise Province|Ise Province]] and attempted to take a boat back to Mutsu to rally his forces against the Ashikaga, but was shipwrecked and died.{{Cite book |last=Sansom |first=George |title=A History of Japan, 1334-1615 |publisher=Stanford University Press |year=1961 |ISBN=0804705259 |page=43,48,66}} In 1824, [[:en:Tōdō Takasawa|Tōdō Takasawa]], the ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of [[:en:Tsu Domain|Tsu Domain]] erected a small shrine on the site of his grave. Following the [[Meiji Restoration]] and the establishment of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]], the [[:en:Government of Meiji Japan|Government of Meiji Japan]] wished to sponsor patriotism by recalling the events and people of the previous Kenmu Restoration. Yūki Shrine was given the official status of a village shrine in 1879 under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]]. [[:en:Emperor Meiji|Emperor Meiji]] visited Mie Prefecture the following year and made an offering of 200 [[:en:Yen|Yen]] to the shrine. The shrine was promoted to special status in 1882 as one of the "Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration". The shrine was burned down in July 1945 during World War II, and was reconstructed in the late 1950s. It is located approximately one kilometer southeast of [[:en:Akogi Station|Akogi Station]] on the [[:en:Kisei Main Line|Kisei Main Line]]. ==See also== *[[List of the Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration|Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== *[http://www.kankomie.or.jp/spot/detail_2924.html Mie Prefectural tourism website] *[https://www.tsukanko.jp/spot/18/ Tsu city tourism website] ]]]] {{Shinto-stub}} {{Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:1879 establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Tsu, Mie]] [[Category:Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] [[Category:Bekkaku Kanpeisha]] [[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] }} [[en:Yūki Shrine]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[en:Yūki Shrine]] [[fr:Yūki-jinja]] [[ja:結城神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11606619}}

Its main festival is held annually on May 1, 2 and 3. It is one of the [[List of the Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration|Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]]. ==History== [[:en:Yūki Munehiro|Yūki Munehiro]] (1266 – 1339) was a vassal of the [[:en:Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura shogunate]] who had been entrusted with southern [[:en:Mutsu Province|Mutsu Province]] and was castellan of [[:en:Shirakawa Castle|Shirakawa Castle]] He was dispatched by [[:en:Hōjō Takatoki|Hōjō Takatoki]] to [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]] in 1331 during the [[:en:Genkō War|Genkō War]], but subsequently defected to the side of [[:en:Emperor Go-Daigo|Emperor Go-Daigo]]. He returned as one of [[Nitta Yoshisada]]'s generals during the 1333 [[:en:Siege of Kamakura (1333)|Battle of Kamakura]] to overthrow the [[:en:Hōjō clan| Hōjō]]. Following the [[Kenmu Restoration]], he served alongside [[Kusunoki Masashige]] as an aide to Emperor Go-Daigo and assisted [[:en:Kitabatake Akiie|Kitabatake Akiie]] conquer Mutsu Province. When [[:en:Ashikaga Takauji|Ashikaga Takauji]] rebelled against Emperor Go-Daigo, he led the army which retook Kyoto from the Ashikaga in 1336 and was awarded a sword from the emperor. However, in 1338 Ashikaga Takauji retook Kyoto, and Yūki Munehiro was forced to flee to [[:en:Yoshino, Nara|Yoshino, Nara]] where Emperor Go-Daigo had established the [[Southern Court]]. He subsequently crossed into [[:en:Ise Province|Ise Province]] and attempted to take a boat back to Mutsu to rally his forces against the Ashikaga, but was shipwrecked and died.{{Cite book |last=Sansom |first=George |title=A History of Japan, 1334-1615 |publisher=Stanford University Press |year=1961 |ISBN=0804705259 |page=43,48,66}} In 1824, [[:en:Tōdō Takasawa|Tōdō Takasawa]], the ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of [[:en:Tsu Domain|Tsu Domain]] erected a small shrine on the site of his grave. Following the [[Meiji Restoration]] and the establishment of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]], the [[:en:Government of Meiji Japan|Government of Meiji Japan]] wished to sponsor patriotism by recalling the events and people of the previous Kenmu Restoration. Yūki Shrine was given the official status of a village shrine in 1879 under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]]. [[:en:Emperor Meiji|Emperor Meiji]] visited Mie Prefecture the following year and made an offering of 200 [[:en:Yen|Yen]] to the shrine. The shrine was promoted to special status in 1882 as one of the "Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration". The shrine was burned down in July 1945 during World War II, and was reconstructed in the late 1950s. It is located approximately one kilometer southeast of [[:en:Akogi Station|Akogi Station]] on the [[:en:Kisei Main Line|Kisei Main Line]]. ==See also== *[[List of the Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration|Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== *[http://www.kankomie.or.jp/spot/detail_2924.html Mie Prefectural tourism website] *[https://www.tsukanko.jp/spot/18/ Tsu city tourism website] ]]]] {{Shinto-stub}} {{Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:1879 establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Tsu, Mie]] [[Category:Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] [[Category:Bekkaku Kanpeisha]] [[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] }} [[en:Yūki Shrine]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[en:Yūki Shrine]] [[fr:Yūki-jinja]] [[ja:結城神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11606619}}

He subsequently crossed into [[:en:Ise Province|Ise Province]] and attempted to take a boat back to Mutsu to rally his forces against the Ashikaga, but was shipwrecked and died.{{Cite book |last=Sansom |first=George |title=A History of Japan, 1334-1615 |publisher=Stanford University Press |year=1961 |ISBN=0804705259 |page=43,48,66}} In 1824, [[:en:Tōdō Takasawa|Tōdō Takasawa]], the ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of [[:en:Tsu Domain|Tsu Domain]] erected a small shrine on the site of his grave. Following the [[Meiji Restoration]] and the establishment of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]], the [[:en:Government of Meiji Japan|Government of Meiji Japan]] wished to sponsor patriotism by recalling the events and people of the previous Kenmu Restoration. Yūki Shrine was given the official status of a village shrine in 1879 under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]]. [[:en:Emperor Meiji|Emperor Meiji]] visited Mie Prefecture the following year and made an offering of 200 [[:en:Yen|Yen]] to the shrine. The shrine was promoted to special status in 1882 as one of the "Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration". The shrine was burned down in July 1945 during World War II, and was reconstructed in the late 1950s. It is located approximately one kilometer southeast of [[:en:Akogi Station|Akogi Station]] on the [[:en:Kisei Main Line|Kisei Main Line]]. ==See also== *[[List of the Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration|Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== *[http://www.kankomie.or.jp/spot/detail_2924.html Mie Prefectural tourism website] *[https://www.tsukanko.jp/spot/18/ Tsu city tourism website] ]]]] {{Shinto-stub}} {{Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:1879 establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Tsu, Mie]] [[Category:Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] [[Category:Bekkaku Kanpeisha]] [[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] }} [[en:Yūki Shrine]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[en:Yūki Shrine]] [[fr:Yūki-jinja]] [[ja:結城神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11606619}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]]

He returned as one of [[Nitta Yoshisada]]'s generals during the 1333 [[:en:Siege of Kamakura (1333)|Battle of Kamakura]] to overthrow the [[:en:Hōjō clan| Hōjō]]. Following the [[Kenmu Restoration]], he served alongside [[Kusunoki Masashige]] as an aide to Emperor Go-Daigo and assisted [[:en:Kitabatake Akiie|Kitabatake Akiie]] conquer Mutsu Province. When [[:en:Ashikaga Takauji|Ashikaga Takauji]] rebelled against Emperor Go-Daigo, he led the army which retook Kyoto from the Ashikaga in 1336 and was awarded a sword from the emperor. However, in 1338 Ashikaga Takauji retook Kyoto, and Yūki Munehiro was forced to flee to [[:en:Yoshino, Nara|Yoshino, Nara]] where Emperor Go-Daigo had established the [[Southern Court]]. He subsequently crossed into [[:en:Ise Province|Ise Province]] and attempted to take a boat back to Mutsu to rally his forces against the Ashikaga, but was shipwrecked and died.{{Cite book |last=Sansom |first=George |title=A History of Japan, 1334-1615 |publisher=Stanford University Press |year=1961 |ISBN=0804705259 |page=43,48,66}} In 1824, [[:en:Tōdō Takasawa|Tōdō Takasawa]], the ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of [[:en:Tsu Domain|Tsu Domain]] erected a small shrine on the site of his grave. Following the [[Meiji Restoration]] and the establishment of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]], the [[:en:Government of Meiji Japan|Government of Meiji Japan]] wished to sponsor patriotism by recalling the events and people of the previous Kenmu Restoration. Yūki Shrine was given the official status of a village shrine in 1879 under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]]. [[:en:Emperor Meiji|Emperor Meiji]] visited Mie Prefecture the following year and made an offering of 200 [[:en:Yen|Yen]] to the shrine. The shrine was promoted to special status in 1882 as one of the "Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration". The shrine was burned down in July 1945 during World War II, and was reconstructed in the late 1950s. It is located approximately one kilometer southeast of [[:en:Akogi Station|Akogi Station]] on the [[:en:Kisei Main Line|Kisei Main Line]]. ==See also== *[[List of the Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration|Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== *[http://www.kankomie.or.jp/spot/detail_2924.html Mie Prefectural tourism website] *[https://www.tsukanko.jp/spot/18/ Tsu city tourism website] ]]]] {{Shinto-stub}} {{Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:1879 establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Tsu, Mie]] [[Category:Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] [[Category:Bekkaku Kanpeisha]] [[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] }} [[en:Yūki Shrine]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[en:Yūki Shrine]] [[fr:Yūki-jinja]] [[ja:結城神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11606619}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] {{wikidata link|Q11606619}}

結城神社 | image = File:Yuukizinnjya.jpg | caption = ''Heiden'' of Yūki Shrine | map_type = Japan Mie Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|34.6977|N|136.515|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Yūki Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[List of the Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration|Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] | deity = [[:en:Yūki Munehiro|Yūki Munehiro]] | founded_by = | established =1824, restored 1879 | date_destroyed = | location = 2341 Fujikata, Tsu City, Mie Prefecture | website = | architecture_style = | festival = May 1-3 | leadership = | location = {{ill|Tsu|WD=Q203027}} }} {{nihongo|'''Yūki Shrine'''|結城神社|Yūki jinja}} is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in the city of [[:en:Tsu, Mie|Tsu]], [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. Its main festival is held annually on May 1, 2 and 3. It is one of the [[List of the Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration|Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]]. ==History== [[:en:Yūki Munehiro|Yūki Munehiro]] (1266 – 1339) was a vassal of the [[:en:Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura shogunate]] who had been entrusted with southern [[:en:Mutsu Province|Mutsu Province]] and was castellan of [[:en:Shirakawa Castle|Shirakawa Castle]] He was dispatched by [[:en:Hōjō Takatoki|Hōjō Takatoki]] to [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]] in 1331 during the [[:en:Genkō War|Genkō War]], but subsequently defected to the side of [[:en:Emperor Go-Daigo|Emperor Go-Daigo]]. He returned as one of [[Nitta Yoshisada]]'s generals during the 1333 [[:en:Siege of Kamakura (1333)|Battle of Kamakura]] to overthrow the [[:en:Hōjō clan| Hōjō]]. Following the [[Kenmu Restoration]], he served alongside [[Kusunoki Masashige]] as an aide to Emperor Go-Daigo and assisted [[:en:Kitabatake Akiie|Kitabatake Akiie]] conquer Mutsu Province. When [[:en:Ashikaga Takauji|Ashikaga Takauji]] rebelled against Emperor Go-Daigo, he led the army which retook Kyoto from the Ashikaga in 1336 and was awarded a sword from the emperor. However, in 1338 Ashikaga Takauji retook Kyoto, and Yūki Munehiro was forced to flee to [[:en:Yoshino, Nara|Yoshino, Nara]] where Emperor Go-Daigo had established the [[Southern Court]]. He subsequently crossed into [[:en:Ise Province|Ise Province]] and attempted to take a boat back to Mutsu to rally his forces against the Ashikaga, but was shipwrecked and died.{{Cite book |last=Sansom |first=George |title=A History of Japan, 1334-1615 |publisher=Stanford University Press |year=1961 |ISBN=0804705259 |page=43,48,66}} In 1824, [[:en:Tōdō Takasawa|Tōdō Takasawa]], the ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of [[:en:Tsu Domain|Tsu Domain]] erected a small shrine on the site of his grave. Following the [[Meiji Restoration]] and the establishment of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]], the [[:en:Government of Meiji Japan|Government of Meiji Japan]] wished to sponsor patriotism by recalling the events and people of the previous Kenmu Restoration. Yūki Shrine was given the official status of a village shrine in 1879 under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]]. [[:en:Emperor Meiji|Emperor Meiji]] visited Mie Prefecture the following year and made an offering of 200 [[:en:Yen|Yen]] to the shrine. The shrine was promoted to special status in 1882 as one of the "Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration". The shrine was burned down in July 1945 during World War II, and was reconstructed in the late 1950s. It is located approximately one kilometer southeast of [[:en:Akogi Station|Akogi Station]] on the [[:en:Kisei Main Line|Kisei Main Line]]. ==See also== *[[List of the Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration|Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== *[http://www.kankomie.or.jp/spot/detail_2924.html Mie Prefectural tourism website] *[https://www.tsukanko.jp/spot/18/ Tsu city tourism website] ]]]] {{Shinto-stub}} {{Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration}} {{draft categories| }} [[en:Yūki Shrine]] [[en:Yūki Shrine]] [[fr:Yūki-jinja]] [[ja:結城神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11606619}} [[Category:1879 establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Bekkaku Kanpeisha]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] [[Category:pages with conflicting qids]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Tsu, Mie]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

He subsequently crossed into [[:en:Ise Province|Ise Province]] and attempted to take a boat back to Mutsu to rally his forces against the Ashikaga, but was shipwrecked and died.{{Cite book |last=Sansom |first=George |title=A History of Japan, 1334-1615 |publisher=Stanford University Press |year=1961 |ISBN=0804705259 |page=43,48,66}} In 1824, [[:en:Tōdō Takasawa|Tōdō Takasawa]], the ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of [[:en:Tsu Domain|Tsu Domain]] erected a small shrine on the site of his grave. [[:en:Emperor Meiji|Emperor Meiji]] visited Mie Prefecture the following year and made an offering of 200 [[:en:Yen|Yen]] to the shrine. The shrine was promoted to special status in 1882 as one of the "Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration". The shrine was burned down in July 1945 during World War II, and was reconstructed in the late 1950s. It is located approximately one kilometer southeast of [[:en:Akogi Station|Akogi Station]] on the [[:en:Kisei Main Line|Kisei Main Line]]. ==See also== *[[List of the Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration|Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== *[http://www.kankomie.or.jp/spot/detail_2924.html Mie Prefectural tourism website] *[https://www.tsukanko.jp/spot/18/ Tsu city tourism website] ]]]] {{Shinto-stub}} {{Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration}} [[en:Yūki Shrine]] [[en:Yūki Shrine]] [[fr:Yūki-jinja]] [[ja:結城神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11606619}} [[Category:1879 establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Bekkaku Kanpeisha]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] [[Category:pages with conflicting qids]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Tsu, Mie]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

結城神社 | image = File:Yuukizinnjya.jpg | caption = ''Heiden'' of Yūki Shrine | map_type = Japan Mie Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|34.6977|N|136.515|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Yūki Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[List of the Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration|Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] | deity = [[:en:Yūki Munehiro|Yūki Munehiro]] | founded_by = | established =1824, restored 1879 | date_destroyed = | location = 2341 Fujikata, Tsu City, Mie Prefecture | website = | architecture_style = | festival = May 1-3 | leadership = | location = {{ill|Tsu|WD=Q203027}} }} {{nihongo|'''Yūki Shrine'''|結城神社|Yūki jinja}} is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in the city of [[:en:Tsu, Mie|Tsu]], [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. Its main festival is held annually on May 1, 2 and 3. It is one of the [[List of the Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration|Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]]. ==History== [[:en:Yūki Munehiro|Yūki Munehiro]] (1266 – 1339) was a vassal of the [[:en:Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura shogunate]] who had been entrusted with southern [[:en:Mutsu Province|Mutsu Province]] and was castellan of [[:en:Shirakawa Castle|Shirakawa Castle]] He was dispatched by [[:en:Hōjō Takatoki|Hōjō Takatoki]] to [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]] in 1331 during the [[:en:Genkō War|Genkō War]], but subsequently defected to the side of [[:en:Emperor Go-Daigo|Emperor Go-Daigo]]. He returned as one of [[Nitta Yoshisada]]'s generals during the 1333 [[:en:Siege of Kamakura (1333)|Battle of Kamakura]] to overthrow the [[:en:Hōjō clan| Hōjō]]. Following the [[Kenmu Restoration]], he served alongside [[Kusunoki Masashige]] as an aide to Emperor Go-Daigo and assisted [[:en:Kitabatake Akiie|Kitabatake Akiie]] conquer Mutsu Province. When [[:en:Ashikaga Takauji|Ashikaga Takauji]] rebelled against Emperor Go-Daigo, he led the army which retook Kyoto from the Ashikaga in 1336 and was awarded a sword from the emperor. However, in 1338 Ashikaga Takauji retook Kyoto, and Yūki Munehiro was forced to flee to [[:en:Yoshino, Nara|Yoshino, Nara]] where Emperor Go-Daigo had established the [[Southern Court]]. He subsequently crossed into [[:en:Ise Province|Ise Province]] and attempted to take a boat back to Mutsu to rally his forces against the Ashikaga, but was shipwrecked and died.{{Cite book |last=Sansom |first=George |title=A History of Japan, 1334-1615 |publisher=Stanford University Press |year=1961 |ISBN=0804705259 |page=43,48,66}} In 1824, [[:en:Tōdō Takasawa|Tōdō Takasawa]], the ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of [[:en:Tsu Domain|Tsu Domain]] erected a small shrine on the site of his grave. Following the [[Meiji Restoration]] and the establishment of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]], the [[:en:Government of Meiji Japan|Government of Meiji Japan]] wished to sponsor patriotism by recalling the events and people of the previous Kenmu Restoration. Yūki Shrine was given the official status of a village shrine in 1879 under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]]. [[:en:Emperor Meiji|Emperor Meiji]] visited Mie Prefecture the following year and made an offering of 200 [[:en:Yen|Yen]] to the shrine. The shrine was promoted to special status in 1882 as one of the "Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration". The shrine was burned down in July 1945 during World War II, and was reconstructed in the late 1950s. It is located approximately one kilometer southeast of [[:en:Akogi Station|Akogi Station]] on the [[:en:Kisei Main Line|Kisei Main Line]]. ==See also== *[[List of the Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration|Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== *[http://www.kankomie.or.jp/spot/detail_2924.html Mie Prefectural tourism website] *[https://www.tsukanko.jp/spot/18/ Tsu city tourism website] ]]]] {{Shinto-stub}} {{Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration}} [[en:Yūki Shrine]] [[en:Yūki Shrine]] [[fr:Yūki-jinja]] [[ja:結城神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11606619}} [[Category:1879 establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Bekkaku Kanpeisha]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] [[Category:Qq]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Tsu, Mie]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

結城神社 | image = File:Yuukizinnjya.jpg | caption = ''Heiden'' of Yūki Shrine | map_type = Japan Mie Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|34.6977|N|136.515|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Yūki Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[List of the Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration|Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] | deity = {{ill|Yūki Munehiro|en|Yūki Munehiro|WD=Q11606570|lt=Yūki Munehiro}} | founded_by = | established =1824, restored 1879 | date_destroyed = | location = 2341 Fujikata, Tsu City, Mie Prefecture | website = | architecture_style = | festival = May 1-3 | leadership = | location = {{ill|Tsu|WD=Q203027}} }} {{nihongo|'''Yūki Shrine'''|結城神社|Yūki jinja}} is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} located in the city of {{ill|Tsu, Mie|en|Tsu, Mie|WD=Q203027|lt=Tsu}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|en|Mie Prefecture|WD=Q128196|lt=Mie Prefecture}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. Its main festival is held annually on May 1, 2 and 3. It is one of the [[List of the Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration|Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]]. ==History== {{ill|Yūki Munehiro|en|Yūki Munehiro|WD=Q11606570|lt=Yūki Munehiro}} (1266 – 1339) was a vassal of the {{ill|Kamakura shogunate|en|Kamakura shogunate|WD=Q736839|lt=Kamakura shogunate}} who had been entrusted with southern {{ill|Mutsu Province|en|Mutsu Province|WD=Q907389|lt=Mutsu Province}} and was castellan of [[:en:Shirakawa Castle|Shirakawa Castle]] He was dispatched by {{ill|Hōjō Takatoki|en|Hōjō Takatoki|WD=Q2355334|lt=Hōjō Takatoki}} to {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto}} in 1331 during the {{ill|Genkō War|en|Genkō War|WD=Q1432624|lt=Genkō War}}, but subsequently defected to the side of {{ill|Emperor Go-Daigo|en|Emperor Go-Daigo|WD=Q313942|lt=Emperor Go-Daigo}}. He returned as one of [[Nitta Yoshisada]]'s generals during the 1333 {{ill|Siege of Kamakura (1333)|en|Siege of Kamakura (1333)|WD=Q7510143|lt=Battle of Kamakura}} to overthrow the {{ill|Hōjō clan|en|Hōjō clan|WD=Q834364|lt= Hōjō}}. Following the [[Kenmu Restoration]], he served alongside [[Kusunoki Masashige]] as an aide to Emperor Go-Daigo and assisted {{ill|Kitabatake Akiie|en|Kitabatake Akiie|WD=Q1036864|lt=Kitabatake Akiie}} conquer Mutsu Province. When {{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|en|Ashikaga Takauji|WD=Q297107|lt=Ashikaga Takauji}} rebelled against Emperor Go-Daigo, he led the army which retook Kyoto from the Ashikaga in 1336 and was awarded a sword from the emperor. However, in 1338 Ashikaga Takauji retook Kyoto, and Yūki Munehiro was forced to flee to {{ill|Yoshino, Nara|en|Yoshino, Nara|WD=Q1203731|lt=Yoshino, Nara}} where Emperor Go-Daigo had established the [[Southern Court]]. He subsequently crossed into {{ill|Ise Province|en|Ise Province|WD=Q864166|lt=Ise Province}} and attempted to take a boat back to Mutsu to rally his forces against the Ashikaga, but was shipwrecked and died.{{Cite book |last=Sansom |first=George |title=A History of Japan, 1334-1615 |publisher=Stanford University Press |year=1961 |ISBN=0804705259 |page=43,48,66}} In 1824, {{ill|Tōdō Takasawa|en|Tōdō Takasawa|WD=Q26213743|lt=Tōdō Takasawa}}, the ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of {{ill|Tsu Domain|en|Tsu Domain|WD=Q1060551|lt=Tsu Domain}} erected a small shrine on the site of his grave. Following the [[Meiji Restoration]] and the establishment of {{ill|State Shinto|en|State Shinto|WD=Q1197248|lt=State Shinto}}, the {{ill|Government of Meiji Japan|en|Government of Meiji Japan|WD=Q1322181|lt=Government of Meiji Japan}} wished to sponsor patriotism by recalling the events and people of the previous Kenmu Restoration. Yūki Shrine was given the official status of a village shrine in 1879 under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]]. {{ill|Emperor Meiji|en|Emperor Meiji|WD=Q174231|lt=Emperor Meiji}} visited Mie Prefecture the following year and made an offering of 200 [[:en:Yen|Yen]] to the shrine. The shrine was promoted to special status in 1882 as one of the "Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration". The shrine was burned down in July 1945 during World War II, and was reconstructed in the late 1950s. It is located approximately one kilometer southeast of {{ill|Akogi Station|en|Akogi Station|WD=Q898582|lt=Akogi Station}} on the {{ill|Kisei Main Line|en|Kisei Main Line|WD=Q582803|lt=Kisei Main Line}}. ==See also== *[[List of the Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration|Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== *[http://www.kankomie.or.jp/spot/detail_2924.html Mie Prefectural tourism website] *[https://www.tsukanko.jp/spot/18/ Tsu city tourism website] ]]]] {{Shinto-stub}} {{Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration}} [[en:Yūki Shrine]] [[en:Yūki Shrine]] [[fr:Yūki-jinja]] [[ja:結城神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11606619}} [[Category:1879 establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Bekkaku Kanpeisha]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] [[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Tsu, Mie]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages with unclosed square brackets]]

It is located approximately one kilometer southeast of {{ill|Akogi Station|en|Akogi Station|WD=Q898582|lt=Akogi Station}} on the {{ill|Kisei Main Line|en|Kisei Main Line|WD=Q582803|lt=Kisei Main Line}}. ==See also== *[[List of the Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration|Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== *[http://www.kankomie.or.jp/spot/detail_2924.html Mie Prefectural tourism website] *[https://www.tsukanko.jp/spot/18/ Tsu city tourism website] ]]]] {{Shinto-stub}} {{Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration}} [[en:Yūki Shrine]] [[en:Yūki Shrine]] [[fr:Yūki-jinja]] [[ja:結城神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11606619}} [[Category:1879 establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Bekkaku Kanpeisha]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] [[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Tsu, Mie]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages with unclosed square brackets]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]]

revision.text:
[[co:commons:Category:Yūki Shrine]][[en:Yūki Shrine]][[fr:Yūki-jinja]][[ja:結城神社]] [[co:commons:Category:Yūki Shrine]][[en:Yūki Shrine]][[fr:Yūki-jinja]][[ja:結城神社]] [[sn:commons:Category:Yūki Shrine]][[en:Yūki Shrine]][[fr:Yūki-jinja]][[ja:結城神社]] {{Infobox religious building | name = Yūki Shrine
結城神社 | image = File:Yuukizinnjya.jpg | caption = ''Heiden'' of Yūki Shrine | map_type = Japan Mie Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|34.6977|N|136.515|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Yūki Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[List of the Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration|Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] | deity = {{ill|Yūki Munehiro|en|Yūki Munehiro|WD=Q11606570|lt=Yūki Munehiro}} | founded_by = | established =1824, restored 1879 | date_destroyed = | location = 2341 Fujikata, Tsu City, Mie Prefecture | website = | architecture_style = | festival = May 1-3 | leadership = | location = {{ill|Tsu|WD=Q203027}} }} {{nihongo|'''Yūki Shrine'''|結城神社|Yūki jinja}} is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} located in the city of {{ill|Tsu, Mie|en|Tsu, Mie|WD=Q203027|lt=Tsu}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|en|Mie Prefecture|WD=Q128196|lt=Mie Prefecture}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. Its main festival is held annually on May 1, 2 and 3. It is one of the [[List of the Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration|Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]]. ==History== {{ill|Yūki Munehiro|en|Yūki Munehiro|WD=Q11606570|lt=Yūki Munehiro}} (1266 – 1339) was a vassal of the {{ill|Kamakura shogunate|en|Kamakura shogunate|WD=Q736839|lt=Kamakura shogunate}} who had been entrusted with southern {{ill|Mutsu Province|en|Mutsu Province|WD=Q907389|lt=Mutsu Province}} and was castellan of [[:en:Shirakawa Castle|Shirakawa Castle]] He was dispatched by {{ill|Hōjō Takatoki|en|Hōjō Takatoki|WD=Q2355334|lt=Hōjō Takatoki}} to {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto}} in 1331 during the {{ill|Genkō War|en|Genkō War|WD=Q1432624|lt=Genkō War}}, but subsequently defected to the side of {{ill|Emperor Go-Daigo|en|Emperor Go-Daigo|WD=Q313942|lt=Emperor Go-Daigo}}. He returned as one of [[Nitta Yoshisada]]'s generals during the 1333 {{ill|Siege of Kamakura (1333)|en|Siege of Kamakura (1333)|WD=Q7510143|lt=Battle of Kamakura}} to overthrow the {{ill|Hōjō clan|en|Hōjō clan|WD=Q834364|lt= Hōjō}}. Following the [[Kenmu Restoration]], he served alongside [[Kusunoki Masashige]] as an aide to Emperor Go-Daigo and assisted {{ill|Kitabatake Akiie|en|Kitabatake Akiie|WD=Q1036864|lt=Kitabatake Akiie}} conquer Mutsu Province. When {{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|en|Ashikaga Takauji|WD=Q297107|lt=Ashikaga Takauji}} rebelled against Emperor Go-Daigo, he led the army which retook Kyoto from the Ashikaga in 1336 and was awarded a sword from the emperor. However, in 1338 Ashikaga Takauji retook Kyoto, and Yūki Munehiro was forced to flee to {{ill|Yoshino, Nara|en|Yoshino, Nara|WD=Q1203731|lt=Yoshino, Nara}} where Emperor Go-Daigo had established the [[Southern Court]]. He subsequently crossed into {{ill|Ise Province|en|Ise Province|WD=Q864166|lt=Ise Province}} and attempted to take a boat back to Mutsu to rally his forces against the Ashikaga, but was shipwrecked and died.{{Cite book |last=Sansom |first=George |title=A History of Japan, 1334-1615 |publisher=Stanford University Press |year=1961 |ISBN=0804705259 |page=43,48,66}} In 1824, {{ill|Tōdō Takasawa|en|Tōdō Takasawa|WD=Q26213743|lt=Tōdō Takasawa}}, the ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of {{ill|Tsu Domain|en|Tsu Domain|WD=Q1060551|lt=Tsu Domain}} erected a small shrine on the site of his grave. Following the [[Meiji Restoration]] and the establishment of {{ill|State Shinto|en|State Shinto|WD=Q1197248|lt=State Shinto}}, the {{ill|Government of Meiji Japan|en|Government of Meiji Japan|WD=Q1322181|lt=Government of Meiji Japan}} wished to sponsor patriotism by recalling the events and people of the previous Kenmu Restoration. Yūki Shrine was given the official status of a village shrine in 1879 under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]]. {{ill|Emperor Meiji|en|Emperor Meiji|WD=Q174231|lt=Emperor Meiji}} visited Mie Prefecture the following year and made an offering of 200 [[:en:Yen|Yen]] to the shrine. The shrine was promoted to special status in 1882 as one of the "Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration". The shrine was burned down in July 1945 during World War II, and was reconstructed in the late 1950s. It is located approximately one kilometer southeast of {{ill|Akogi Station|en|Akogi Station|WD=Q898582|lt=Akogi Station}} on the {{ill|Kisei Main Line|en|Kisei Main Line|WD=Q582803|lt=Kisei Main Line}}. ==See also== *[[List of the Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration|Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== *[http://www.kankomie.or.jp/spot/detail_2924.html Mie Prefectural tourism website] *[https://www.tsukanko.jp/spot/18/ Tsu city tourism website] ]]]] {{Shinto-stub}} {{Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration}} [[en:Yūki Shrine]] [[en:Yūki Shrine]] [[fr:Yūki-jinja]] [[ja:結城神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11606619}} [[Category:1879 establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Bekkaku Kanpeisha]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] [[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Tsu, Mie]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages with unclosed square brackets]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]]

He subsequently crossed into {{ill|Ise Province|en|Ise Province|WD=Q864166|lt=Ise Province|1=Ise Province}} and attempted to take a boat back to Mutsu to rally his forces against the Ashikaga, but was shipwrecked and died.{{Cite book |last=Sansom |first=George |title=A History of Japan, 1334-1615 |publisher=Stanford University Press |year=1961 |ISBN=0804705259 |page=43,48,66}} In 1824, {{ill|Tōdō Takasawa|en|Tōdō Takasawa|WD=Q26213743|lt=Tōdō Takasawa|1=Tōdō Takasawa}}, the ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of {{ill|Tsu Domain|en|Tsu Domain|WD=Q1060551|lt=Tsu Domain|1=Tsu Domain}} erected a small shrine on the site of his grave. Following the [[Meiji Restoration]] and the establishment of {{ill|State Shinto|en|State Shinto|WD=Q1197248|lt=State Shinto|1=State Shinto}}, the {{ill|Government of Meiji Japan|en|Government of Meiji Japan|WD=Q1322181|lt=Government of Meiji Japan|1=Government of Meiji Japan}} wished to sponsor patriotism by recalling the events and people of the previous Kenmu Restoration. Yūki Shrine was given the official status of a village shrine in 1879 under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]]. {{ill|Emperor Meiji|en|Emperor Meiji|WD=Q174231|lt=Emperor Meiji|1=Emperor Meiji}} visited Mie Prefecture the following year and made an offering of 200 [[:en:Yen|Yen]] to the shrine. The shrine was promoted to special status in 1882 as one of the "Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration". The shrine was burned down in July 1945 during World War II, and was reconstructed in the late 1950s. It is located approximately one kilometer southeast of {{ill|Akogi Station|en|Akogi Station|WD=Q898582|lt=Akogi Station|1=Akogi Station}} on the {{ill|Kisei Main Line|en|Kisei Main Line|WD=Q582803|lt=Kisei Main Line|1=Kisei Main Line}}. ==See also== *[[List of the Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration|Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== *[http://www.kankomie.or.jp/spot/detail_2924.html Mie Prefectural tourism website] *[https://www.tsukanko.jp/spot/18/ Tsu city tourism website] ]]]] {{Shinto-stub}} {{Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration}} [[en:Yūki Shrine]] [[en:Yūki Shrine]] [[fr:Yūki-jinja]] [[ja:結城神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11606619}} [[Category:1879 establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Bekkaku Kanpeisha]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] [[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Tsu, Mie]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages with unclosed square brackets]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:pages]]

結城神社 | image = File:Yuukizinnjya.jpg | caption = ''Heiden'' of Yūki Shrine | map_type = Japan Mie Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|34.6977|N|136.515|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Yūki Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[List of the Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration|Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] | deity = {{ill|Yūki Munehiro|en|Yūki Munehiro|WD=Q11606570|lt=Yūki Munehiro|1=Yūki Munehiro}} | founded_by = | established =1824, restored 1879 | date_destroyed = | location = 2341 Fujikata, Tsu City, Mie Prefecture | website = | architecture_style = | festival = May 1-3 | leadership = | location = {{ill|Tsu|WD=Q203027|1=Tsu, Mie}} }} {{nihongo|'''Yūki Shrine'''|結城神社|Yūki jinja}} is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} located in the city of {{ill|Tsu, Mie|en|Tsu, Mie|WD=Q203027|lt=Tsu|1=Tsu, Mie}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|en|Mie Prefecture|WD=Q128196|lt=Mie Prefecture|1=Mie Prefecture}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. Its main festival is held annually on May 1, 2 and 3. It is one of the [[List of the Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration|Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]]. ==History== {{ill|Yūki Munehiro|en|Yūki Munehiro|WD=Q11606570|lt=Yūki Munehiro|1=Yūki Munehiro}} (1266 – 1339) was a vassal of the {{ill|Kamakura shogunate|en|Kamakura shogunate|WD=Q736839|lt=Kamakura shogunate|1=Kamakura shogunate}} who had been entrusted with southern {{ill|Mutsu Province|en|Mutsu Province|WD=Q907389|lt=Mutsu Province|1=Mutsu Province}} and was castellan of [[:en:Shirakawa Castle|Shirakawa Castle]] He was dispatched by {{ill|Hōjō Takatoki|en|Hōjō Takatoki|WD=Q2355334|lt=Hōjō Takatoki|1=Hōjō Takatoki}} to {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}} in 1331 during the {{ill|Genkō War|en|Genkō War|WD=Q1432624|lt=Genkō War|1=Genkō War}}, but subsequently defected to the side of {{ill|Emperor Go-Daigo|en|Emperor Go-Daigo|WD=Q313942|lt=Emperor Go-Daigo|1=Emperor Go-Daigo}}. He returned as one of [[Nitta Yoshisada]]'s generals during the 1333 {{ill|Siege of Kamakura (1333)|en|Siege of Kamakura (1333)|WD=Q7510143|lt=Battle of Kamakura|1=Siege of Kamakura (1333)}} to overthrow the {{ill|Hōjō clan|en|Hōjō clan|WD=Q834364|lt= Hōjō|1=Hōjō clan}}. Following the [[Kenmu Restoration]], he served alongside [[Kusunoki Masashige]] as an aide to Emperor Go-Daigo and assisted {{ill|Kitabatake Akiie|en|Kitabatake Akiie|WD=Q1036864|lt=Kitabatake Akiie|1=Kitabatake Akiie}} conquer Mutsu Province. When {{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|en|Ashikaga Takauji|WD=Q297107|lt=Ashikaga Takauji|1=Ashikaga Takauji}} rebelled against Emperor Go-Daigo, he led the army which retook Kyoto from the Ashikaga in 1336 and was awarded a sword from the emperor. However, in 1338 Ashikaga Takauji retook Kyoto, and Yūki Munehiro was forced to flee to {{ill|Yoshino, Nara|en|Yoshino, Nara|WD=Q1203731|lt=Yoshino, Nara|1=Yoshino, Nara}} where Emperor Go-Daigo had established the [[Southern Court]]. He subsequently crossed into {{ill|Ise Province|en|Ise Province|WD=Q864166|lt=Ise Province|1=Ise Province}} and attempted to take a boat back to Mutsu to rally his forces against the Ashikaga, but was shipwrecked and died.{{Cite book |last=Sansom |first=George |title=A History of Japan, 1334-1615 |publisher=Stanford University Press |year=1961 |ISBN=0804705259 |page=43,48,66}} In 1824, {{ill|Tōdō Takasawa|en|Tōdō Takasawa|WD=Q26213743|lt=Tōdō Takasawa|1=Tōdō Takasawa}}, the ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of {{ill|Tsu Domain|en|Tsu Domain|WD=Q1060551|lt=Tsu Domain|1=Tsu Domain}} erected a small shrine on the site of his grave. Following the [[Meiji Restoration]] and the establishment of {{ill|State Shinto|en|State Shinto|WD=Q1197248|lt=State Shinto|1=State Shinto}}, the {{ill|Government of Meiji Japan|en|Government of Meiji Japan|WD=Q1322181|lt=Government of Meiji Japan|1=Government of Meiji Japan}} wished to sponsor patriotism by recalling the events and people of the previous Kenmu Restoration. Yūki Shrine was given the official status of a village shrine in 1879 under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]]. {{ill|Emperor Meiji|en|Emperor Meiji|WD=Q174231|lt=Emperor Meiji|1=Emperor Meiji}} visited Mie Prefecture the following year and made an offering of 200 [[:en:Yen|Yen]] to the shrine. The shrine was promoted to special status in 1882 as one of the "Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration". The shrine was burned down in July 1945 during World War II, and was reconstructed in the late 1950s. It is located approximately one kilometer southeast of {{ill|Akogi Station|en|Akogi Station|WD=Q898582|lt=Akogi Station|1=Akogi Station}} on the {{ill|Kisei Main Line|en|Kisei Main Line|WD=Q582803|lt=Kisei Main Line|1=Kisei Main Line}}. ==See also== *[[List of the Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration|Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== *[http://www.kankomie.or.jp/spot/detail_2924.html Mie Prefectural tourism website] *[https://www.tsukanko.jp/spot/18/ Tsu city tourism website] ]]]] {{Shinto-stub}} {{Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration}} [[en:Yūki Shrine]] [[en:Yūki Shrine]] [[fr:Yūki-jinja]] [[ja:結城神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11606619}} [[Category:1879 establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Bekkaku Kanpeisha]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] [[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Tsu, Mie]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages with unclosed square brackets]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:pages]]

結城神社 | image = File:Yuukizinnjya.jpg | caption = ''Heiden'' of Yūki Shrine | map_type = Japan Mie Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|34.6977|N|136.515|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Yūki Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[List of the Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration|Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] | deity = {{ill|Yūki Munehiro|en|Yūki Munehiro|WD=Q11606570|lt=Yūki Munehiro|1=Yūki Munehiro}} | founded_by = | established =1824, restored 1879 | date_destroyed = | location = 2341 Fujikata, Tsu City, Mie Prefecture | website = | architecture_style = | festival = May 1-3 | leadership = | location = {{ill|Tsu|WD=Q203027|1=Tsu, Mie}} }} {{nihongo|'''Yūki Shrine'''|結城神社|Yūki jinja}} is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} located in the city of {{ill|Tsu, Mie|en|Tsu, Mie|WD=Q203027|lt=Tsu|1=Tsu, Mie}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|en|Mie Prefecture|WD=Q128196|lt=Mie Prefecture|1=Mie Prefecture}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. Its main festival is held annually on May 1, 2 and 3. It is one of the [[List of the Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration|Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]]. ==History== {{ill|Yūki Munehiro|en|Yūki Munehiro|WD=Q11606570|lt=Yūki Munehiro|1=Yūki Munehiro}} (1266 – 1339) was a vassal of the {{ill|Kamakura shogunate|en|Kamakura shogunate|WD=Q736839|lt=Kamakura shogunate|1=Kamakura shogunate}} who had been entrusted with southern {{ill|Mutsu Province|en|Mutsu Province|WD=Q907389|lt=Mutsu Province|1=Mutsu Province}} and was castellan of [[:en:Shirakawa Castle|Shirakawa Castle]] He was dispatched by {{ill|Hōjō Takatoki|en|Hōjō Takatoki|WD=Q2355334|lt=Hōjō Takatoki|1=Hōjō Takatoki}} to {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}} in 1331 during the {{ill|Genkō War|en|Genkō War|WD=Q1432624|lt=Genkō War|1=Genkō War}}, but subsequently defected to the side of {{ill|Emperor Go-Daigo|en|Emperor Go-Daigo|WD=Q313942|lt=Emperor Go-Daigo|1=Emperor Go-Daigo}}. He returned as one of [[Nitta Yoshisada]]'s generals during the 1333 {{ill|Siege of Kamakura (1333)|en|Siege of Kamakura (1333)|WD=Q7510143|lt=Battle of Kamakura|1=Siege of Kamakura (1333)}} to overthrow the {{ill|Hōjō clan|en|Hōjō clan|WD=Q834364|lt= Hōjō|1=Hōjō clan}}. Following the [[Kenmu Restoration]], he served alongside [[Kusunoki Masashige]] as an aide to Emperor Go-Daigo and assisted {{ill|Kitabatake Akiie|en|Kitabatake Akiie|WD=Q1036864|lt=Kitabatake Akiie|1=Kitabatake Akiie}} conquer Mutsu Province. When {{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|en|Ashikaga Takauji|WD=Q297107|lt=Ashikaga Takauji|1=Ashikaga Takauji}} rebelled against Emperor Go-Daigo, he led the army which retook Kyoto from the Ashikaga in 1336 and was awarded a sword from the emperor. However, in 1338 Ashikaga Takauji retook Kyoto, and Yūki Munehiro was forced to flee to {{ill|Yoshino, Nara|en|Yoshino, Nara|WD=Q1203731|lt=Yoshino, Nara|1=Yoshino, Nara}} where Emperor Go-Daigo had established the [[Southern Court]]. He subsequently crossed into {{ill|Ise Province|en|Ise Province|WD=Q864166|lt=Ise Province|1=Ise Province}} and attempted to take a boat back to Mutsu to rally his forces against the Ashikaga, but was shipwrecked and died.{{Cite book |last=Sansom |first=George |title=A History of Japan, 1334-1615 |publisher=Stanford University Press |year=1961 |ISBN=0804705259 |page=43,48,66}} In 1824, {{ill|Tōdō Takasawa|en|Tōdō Takasawa|WD=Q26213743|lt=Tōdō Takasawa|1=Tōdō Takasawa}}, the ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of {{ill|Tsu Domain|en|Tsu Domain|WD=Q1060551|lt=Tsu Domain|1=Tsu Domain}} erected a small shrine on the site of his grave. Following the [[Meiji Restoration]] and the establishment of {{ill|State Shinto|en|State Shinto|WD=Q1197248|lt=State Shinto|1=State Shinto}}, the {{ill|Government of Meiji Japan|en|Government of Meiji Japan|WD=Q1322181|lt=Government of Meiji Japan|1=Government of Meiji Japan}} wished to sponsor patriotism by recalling the events and people of the previous Kenmu Restoration. Yūki Shrine was given the official status of a village shrine in 1879 under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]]. {{ill|Emperor Meiji|en|Emperor Meiji|WD=Q174231|lt=Emperor Meiji|1=Emperor Meiji}} visited Mie Prefecture the following year and made an offering of 200 [[:en:Yen|Yen]] to the shrine. The shrine was promoted to special status in 1882 as one of the "Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration". The shrine was burned down in July 1945 during World War II, and was reconstructed in the late 1950s. It is located approximately one kilometer southeast of {{ill|Akogi Station|en|Akogi Station|WD=Q898582|lt=Akogi Station|1=Akogi Station}} on the {{ill|Kisei Main Line|en|Kisei Main Line|WD=Q582803|lt=Kisei Main Line|1=Kisei Main Line}}. ==See also== *[[List of the Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration|Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== *[http://www.kankomie.or.jp/spot/detail_2924.html Mie Prefectural tourism website] *[https://www.tsukanko.jp/spot/18/ Tsu city tourism website] ]]]] {{Shinto-stub}} {{Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration}} [[en:Yūki Shrine]] [[en:Yūki Shrine]] [[fr:Yūki-jinja]] [[ja:結城神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11606619}} [[Category:1879 establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Bekkaku Kanpeisha]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] [[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Tsu, Mie]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]
